verses.explainer: But you are to seek the place the Lord your God will choose from among all your tribes to put his Name there for his dwelling. To that place you must go.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Kings 8:29 -- Solomon identifies the Jerusalem temple as the place where God chose to put his Name: 'My eyes and my heart will always be there.'
crossReferences.connection: 1 Kings 8:29 -- Solomon identifies the Jerusalem temple as the place where God chose to put his Name: 'My eyes and my heart will always be there.'

verses.text: There you shall bring your burnt offerings, your sacrifices, your tithes, the wave offering of your hand, your vows, your freewill offerings, and the firstborn of your herd and of your flock.
verses.explainer: There bring your burnt offerings and sacrifices, your tithes and special gifts, what you have vowed to give and your freewill offerings, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks.

verses.text: There you shall eat before Yahweh your God, and you shall rejoice in all that you put your hand to, you and your households, in which Yahweh your God has blessed you.
verses.explainer: There, in the presence of the Lord your God, you and your families shall eat and shall rejoice in everything you have put your hand to, because the Lord your God has blessed you.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Worship in Deuteronomy is explicitly joyful. The word 'rejoice' appears frequently in chapters 12-26. Worship is not grim duty but celebratory feasting in God's presence.

verses.text: You shall not do all the things that we do here today, every man whatever is right in his own eyes;
verses.explainer: You are not to do as we do here today, everyone doing as they see fit.

verses.text: for you haven’t yet come to the rest and to the inheritance, which Yahweh your God gives you.
verses.explainer: For you have not yet reached the resting place and the inheritance the Lord your God is giving you.

verses.text: But when you go over the Jordan, and dwell in the land which Yahweh your God causes you to inherit, and he gives you rest from all your enemies around you, so that you dwell in safety;
verses.explainer: But you will cross the Jordan and settle in the land the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance, and he will give you rest from all your enemies around you so that you will live in safety.

verses.text: then it shall happen that to the place which Yahweh your God shall choose, to cause his name to dwell there, there you shall bring all that I command you: your burnt offerings, your sacrifices, your tithes, the wave offering of your hand, and all your choice vows which you vow to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Then to the place the Lord your God will choose as a dwelling for his Name -- there you are to bring everything I command you: your burnt offerings and sacrifices, your tithes and special gifts, and all the choice possessions you have vowed to the Lord.

verses.text: You shall rejoice before Yahweh your God: you, and your sons, your daughters, your male servants, your female servants, and the Levite who is within your gates, because he has no portion nor inheritance with you.
verses.explainer: And there rejoice before the Lord your God -- you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levites from your towns who have no allotment or inheritance of their own.

verses.text: Be careful that you don’t offer your burnt offerings in every place that you see;
verses.explainer: Be careful not to sacrifice your burnt offerings anywhere you please.

verses.text: but in the place which Yahweh chooses in one of your tribes, there you shall offer your burnt offerings, and there you shall do all that I command you.
verses.explainer: Offer them only at the place the Lord will choose in one of your tribes, and there observe everything I command you.

verses.text: Notwithstanding, you may kill and eat meat within all your gates, after all the desire of your soul, according to Yahweh your God’s blessing which he has given you. The unclean and the clean may eat of it, as of the gazelle, and as of the deer.
verses.explainer: Nevertheless, you may slaughter your animals in any of your towns and eat as much of the meat as you want, as if it were gazelle or deer, according to the blessing the Lord your God gives you. Both the ceremonially unclean and the clean may eat it.

verses.text: Only you shall not eat the blood. You shall pour it out on the earth like water.
verses.explainer: But you must not eat the blood; pour it out on the ground like water.

crossReferences.reference: Genesis 9:4 -- The prohibition against consuming blood goes back to Noah: 'You must not eat meat that has its lifeblood still in it.' Blood represents life and belongs to God.
crossReferences.connection: Genesis 9:4 -- The prohibition against consuming blood goes back to Noah: 'You must not eat meat that has its lifeblood still in it.' Blood represents life and belongs to God.

verses.text: You may not eat within your gates the tithe of your grain, or of your new wine, or of your oil, or the firstborn of your herd or of your flock, nor any of your vows which you vow, nor your freewill offerings, nor the wave offering of your hand;
verses.explainer: You must not eat in your own towns the tithe of your grain, new wine, or olive oil, the firstborn of your herds and flocks, or whatever you have vowed to give, your freewill offerings or special gifts.

verses.text: but you shall eat them before Yahweh your God in the place which Yahweh your God shall choose: you, your son, your daughter, your male servant, your female servant, and the Levite who is within your gates. You shall rejoice before Yahweh your God in all that you put your hand to.
verses.explainer: Instead, you are to eat them in the presence of the Lord your God at the place the Lord will choose -- you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levites. Rejoice before the Lord in everything you put your hand to.

verses.text: Be careful that you don’t forsake the Levite as long as you live in your land.
verses.explainer: Be careful not to neglect the Levites as long as you live in your land.

verses.text: When Yahweh your God enlarges your border, as he has promised you, and you say, “I want to eat meat,” because your soul desires to eat meat; you may eat meat, after all the desire of your soul.
verses.explainer: When the Lord your God has enlarged your territory as he promised you, and you crave meat and say, 'I would like some meat,' then you may eat as much of it as you want.

verses.text: If the place which Yahweh your God shall choose, to put his name there, is too far from you, then you shall kill of your herd and of your flock, which Yahweh has given you, as I have commanded you; and you may eat within your gates, after all the desire of your soul.
verses.explainer: If the place where the Lord your God chooses to put his Name is too far away from you, you may slaughter animals from the herds and flocks the Lord has given you.

verses.text: Even as the gazelle and as the deer is eaten, so you shall eat of it. The unclean and the clean may eat of it alike.
verses.explainer: Eat them in your own towns as you would gazelle or deer. Both the ceremonially unclean and the clean may eat.

verses.text: Only be sure that you don’t eat the blood; for the blood is the life. You shall not eat the life with the meat.
verses.explainer: But be sure you do not eat the blood, because the blood is the life, and you must not eat the life with the meat.

verses.text: You shall not eat it. You shall pour it out on the earth like water.
verses.explainer: You must not eat the blood; pour it out on the ground like water.

verses.text: You shall not eat it; that it may go well with you, and with your children after you, when you shall do that which is right in Yahweh’s eyes.
verses.explainer: Do not eat it, so that it may go well with you and your children after you, because you will be doing what is right in the eyes of the Lord.

verses.text: Only your holy things which you have, and your vows, you shall take, and go to the place which Yahweh shall choose.
verses.explainer: But take your consecrated things and whatever you have vowed to give, and go to the place the Lord will choose.

verses.text: You shall offer your burnt offerings, the meat and the blood, on Yahweh your God’s altar; and the blood of your sacrifices shall be poured out on Yahweh your God’s altar; and you shall eat the meat.
verses.explainer: Present your burnt offerings on the altar of the Lord your God, both the meat and the blood. The blood of your sacrifices must be poured beside the altar of the Lord your God, but you may eat the meat.

verses.text: Observe and hear all these words which I command you, that it may go well with you, and with your children after you forever, when you do that which is good and right in Yahweh your God’s eyes.
verses.explainer: Be careful to obey all these regulations I am giving you, so that it may always go well with you and your children after you, because you will be doing what is good and right in the eyes of the Lord your God.

verses.text: When Yahweh your God cuts off the nations from before you, where you go in to dispossess them, and you dispossess them, and dwell in their land;
verses.explainer: The Lord your God will cut off before you the nations you are about to invade and dispossess. But when you have driven them out and settled in their land --

verses.text: be careful that you are not ensnared to follow them, after that they are destroyed from before you; and that you not inquire after their gods, saying, “How do these nations serve their gods? I will do likewise.”
verses.explainer: Be careful not to be ensnared by inquiring about their gods, saying, 'How do these nations serve their gods? We will do the same.'

verses.text: You shall not do so to Yahweh your God; for every abomination to Yahweh, which he hates, they have done to their gods; for they even burn their sons and their daughters in the fire to their gods.
verses.explainer: You must not worship the Lord your God in their way, because in worshiping their gods, they do all kinds of detestable things the Lord hates. They even burn their sons and daughters in the fire as sacrifices to their gods.

verses.text: Whatever thing I command you, that you shall observe to do. You shall not add to it, nor take away from it.
verses.explainer: See that you do all I command you; do not add to it or take away from it.

explainer.summary: Three scenarios of idolatrous temptation are addressed: a prophet who performs signs but leads to other gods, a family member who secretly entices to idolatry, and an entire town that turns to worship other gods. In each case, the punishment is severe -- death for the individual, destruction for the town. The chapter establishes that no relationship, no miraculous sign, and no communal consensus can justify departing from the Lord.
themes: False prophets tested by their teaching, not their miracles
No relationship overrides covenant loyalty
Entire towns devoted to destruction for apostasy
Idolatry as the capital offense of the covenant
Testing whether Israel truly loves God
explainer.contextInBook: After establishing centralized worship (chapter 12), Moses addresses the greatest threats to that worship: prophets, family members, and whole communities that might lead Israel away from the Lord. The chapter moves from external threat to intimate threat to communal threat.

wordStudies.significance: God uses false prophets as a 'test' (v.3) to determine whether Israel truly loves God with all their heart. The test is whether miraculous signs can override theological truth. The answer must always be no -- truth trumps signs.

verses.text: If a prophet or a dreamer of dreams arises among you, and he gives you a sign or a wonder,
verses.explainer: If a prophet, or one who foretells by dreams, appears among you and announces a sign or wonder to you --

verses.text: and the sign or the wonder comes to pass, of which he spoke to you, saying, “Let us go after other gods” (which you have not known) “and let us serve them”;
verses.explainer: And if the sign or wonder spoken of takes place, and the prophet says, 'Let us follow other gods' and 'Let us worship them' --

verses.text: you shall not listen to the words of that prophet, or to that dreamer of dreams; for Yahweh your God is testing you, to know whether you love Yahweh your God with all your heart and with all your soul.
verses.explainer: You must not listen to the words of that prophet. The Lord your God is testing you to find out whether you love him with all your heart and with all your soul.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 7:22-23 -- Jesus warns: 'Many will say to me on that day, Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name and perform many miracles? Then I will tell them plainly, I never knew you.'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 7:22-23 -- Jesus warns: 'Many will say to me on that day, Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name and perform many miracles? Then I will tell them plainly, I never knew you.'

verses.text: You shall walk after Yahweh your God, fear him, keep his commandments, and obey his voice, and you shall serve him, and cling to him.
verses.explainer: It is the Lord your God you must follow, and him you must revere. Keep his commands and obey him; serve him and hold fast to him.

verses.text: That prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death, because he has spoken rebellion against Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, and redeemed you out of the house of bondage, to draw you aside out of the way which Yahweh your God commanded you to walk in. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: That prophet or dreamer must be put to death for inciting rebellion against the Lord your God. You must purge the evil from among you.

thenVsNow.then: The death penalty for false prophecy protected the community from the most dangerous form of deception -- spiritual manipulation that comes with supernatural credentials.
thenVsNow.now: The principle endures: theological truth is the standard by which prophetic claims are evaluated, not impressive experiences or signs. Discernment requires testing messages against established revelation.

verses.text: If your brother, the son of your mother, or your son, or your daughter, or the wife of your bosom, or your friend, who is as your own soul, entices you secretly, saying, “Let us go and serve other gods,” which you have not known, you, nor your fathers;
verses.explainer: If your very own brother, or your son or daughter, or the wife you love, or your closest friend secretly entices you, saying, 'Let us go and worship other gods' --

verses.text: of the gods of the peoples who are around you, near to you, or far off from you, from the one end of the earth even to the other end of the earth;
verses.explainer: Gods that neither you nor your ancestors have known, gods of the peoples around you, whether near or far, from one end of the land to the other --

verses.text: you shall not consent to him, nor listen to him; neither shall your eye pity him, neither shall you spare, neither shall you conceal him;
verses.explainer: Do not yield to them or listen to them. Show them no pity. Do not spare them or shield them.

verses.text: but you shall surely kill him. Your hand shall be first on him to put him to death, and afterwards the hands of all the people.
verses.explainer: You must certainly put them to death. Your hand must be the first in putting them to death, and then the hands of all the people.

verses.text: You shall stone him to death with stones, because he has sought to draw you away from Yahweh your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
verses.explainer: Stone them to death, because they tried to turn you away from the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery.

verses.text: All Israel shall hear, and fear, and shall not do any more wickedness like this among you.
verses.explainer: Then all Israel will hear and be afraid, and no one among you will do such an evil thing again.

verses.text: If you shall hear about one of your cities, which Yahweh your God gives you to dwell there, that
verses.explainer: If you hear it said about one of the towns the Lord your God is giving you to live in --

verses.text: certain wicked fellows have gone out from among you, and have drawn away the inhabitants of their city, saying, “Let us go and serve other gods,” which you have not known;
verses.explainer: That troublemakers have arisen among you and have led the people of their town astray, saying, 'Let us go and worship other gods' --

verses.text: then you shall inquire, and make search, and ask diligently. Behold, if it is true, and the thing certain, that such abomination was done among you,
verses.explainer: Then you must inquire, probe, and investigate it thoroughly. And if it is true and it has been proved that this detestable thing has been done among you --

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The requirement to 'inquire, probe, and investigate thoroughly' before acting establishes due process even in cases of communal apostasy. No town can be destroyed on rumor alone -- evidence must be verified.

verses.text: you shall surely strike the inhabitants of that city with the edge of the sword, destroying it utterly, with all that is therein and its livestock, with the edge of the sword.
verses.explainer: You must certainly put to the sword all who live in that town. You must destroy it completely, both its people and its livestock.

verses.text: You shall gather all its plunder into the middle of its street, and shall burn with fire the city, and all of its plunder, to Yahweh your God. It shall be a heap forever. It shall not be built again.
verses.explainer: You are to gather all the plunder of the town into the middle of the public square and completely burn the town and all its plunder as a whole burnt offering to the Lord your God. That town is to remain a ruin forever, never to be rebuilt.

verses.text: Nothing of the devoted thing shall cling to your hand, that Yahweh may turn from the fierceness of his anger, and show you mercy, and have compassion on you, and multiply you, as he has sworn to your fathers;
verses.explainer: None of the condemned things are to be found in your hands. Then the Lord will turn from his fierce anger, will show you mercy, and will have compassion on you.

verses.text: when you listen to Yahweh your God’s voice, to keep all his commandments which I command you today, to do that which is right in Yahweh your God’s eyes.
verses.explainer: He will increase your numbers, as he promised on oath to your ancestors -- because you obey the Lord your God by keeping all his commands that I am giving you today and doing what is right in his eyes.

explainer.summary: Moses prohibits pagan mourning practices, then restates the dietary laws distinguishing clean from unclean animals (paralleling Leviticus 11). The chapter concludes with instructions about tithing: a tenth of all produce is to be eaten in celebration at the central sanctuary, and every third year the tithe supports Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows.
themes: Israel's holiness expressed in daily life
Dietary laws as identity markers
Tithing as joyful celebration
The third-year tithe for the vulnerable
You are children of the Lord
explainer.contextInBook: After addressing worship (12) and idolatry (13), Deuteronomy moves to daily life. Dietary laws and tithing practices shape everyday identity -- what you eat and how you use your resources define who you are.

wordStudies.significance: The tithe (one-tenth) was not merely a tax but a celebration. The worshiper ate the tithe in God's presence with joy. Every third year, however, the tithe stayed local and was distributed to the vulnerable -- making the tithe both worship and social welfare.

verses.text: You are the children of Yahweh your God. You shall not cut yourselves, nor make any baldness between your eyes for the dead.
verses.explainer: You are the children of the Lord your God. Do not cut yourselves or shave the front of your heads for the dead.

verses.text: For you are a holy people to Yahweh your God, and Yahweh has chosen you to be a people for his own possession, above all peoples who are on the face of the earth.
verses.explainer: For you are a people holy to the Lord your God. Out of all the peoples on the face of the earth, the Lord has chosen you to be his treasured possession.

verses.text: You shall not eat any abominable thing.
verses.explainer: Do not eat any detestable thing.

verses.text: These are the animals which you may eat: the ox, the sheep, the goat,
verses.explainer: These are the animals you may eat: the ox, the sheep, the goat.

verses.text: the deer, the gazelle, the roebuck, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the chamois.
verses.explainer: The deer, the gazelle, the roe deer, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep.

verses.text: Every animal that parts the hoof, and has the hoof cloven in two and chews the cud, among the animals, that may you eat.
verses.explainer: You may eat any animal that has a divided hoof and that chews the cud.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The dual requirements of split hooves and cud-chewing created a clear, practical classification system. These criteria excluded animals that met only one requirement, making the boundaries unambiguous.

verses.text: Nevertheless these you shall not eat of them that chew the cud, or of those who have the hoof cloven: the camel, the hare, and the rabbit. Because they chew the cud but don’t part the hoof, they are unclean to you.
verses.explainer: However, of those that chew the cud or have a divided hoof you may not eat the camel, the rabbit, or the hyrax. Although they chew the cud, they do not have a divided hoof; they are ceremonially unclean for you.

verses.text: The pig, because it has a split hoof but doesn’t chew the cud, is unclean to you. You shall not eat their meat, and you shall not touch their carcasses.
verses.explainer: The pig is also unclean; although it has a divided hoof, it does not chew the cud. You are not to eat their meat or touch their carcasses.

thenVsNow.then: The pig was particularly significant because it was common in Canaanite diet and ritual. Avoiding pork became one of the most visible markers of Israelite identity.
thenVsNow.now: Whether Christians should observe these dietary laws has been debated since Acts 10 (Peter's vision) and Acts 15 (the Jerusalem Council). Most Christian traditions consider the dietary laws fulfilled in Christ, while recognizing that they taught important principles about holiness and distinction.

verses.text: These you may eat of all that are in the waters: whatever has fins and scales may you eat.
verses.explainer: Of all the creatures living in the water, you may eat any that has fins and scales.

verses.text: You shall not eat whatever doesn’t have fins and scales. It is unclean to you.
verses.explainer: But anything that does not have fins and scales you may not eat; for you it is unclean.

verses.text: Of all clean birds you may eat.
verses.explainer: You may eat any clean bird.

verses.text: But these are they of which you shall not eat: the eagle, the vulture, the osprey,
verses.explainer: But these you may not eat: the eagle, the vulture, the black vulture.

verses.text: the red kite, the falcon, the kite after its kind,
verses.explainer: The red kite, the black kite, any kind of falcon.

verses.text: every raven after its kind,
verses.explainer: Any kind of raven.

verses.text: the ostrich, the owl, the seagull, the hawk after its kind,
verses.explainer: The horned owl, the screech owl, the gull, any kind of hawk.

verses.text: the little owl, the great owl, the horned owl,
verses.explainer: The little owl, the great owl, the white owl.

verses.text: the pelican, the vulture, the cormorant,
verses.explainer: The desert owl, the osprey, the cormorant.

verses.text: the stork, the heron after its kind, the hoopoe, and the bat.
verses.explainer: The stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.

verses.text: All winged creeping things are unclean to you. They shall not be eaten.
verses.explainer: All flying insects are unclean to you; do not eat them.

verses.text: Of all clean birds you may eat.
verses.explainer: But any winged creature that is clean you may eat.

verses.text: You shall not eat of anything that dies of itself. You may give it to the foreigner living among you who is within your gates, that he may eat it; or you may sell it to a foreigner; for you are a holy people to Yahweh your God. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother’s milk.
verses.explainer: Do not eat anything you find already dead. You may give it to the foreigner residing in any of your towns, or you may sell it to any other foreigner. But you are a people holy to the Lord your God. Do not cook a young goat in its mother's milk.

verses.text: You shall surely tithe all the increase of your seed, that which comes out of the field year by year.
verses.explainer: Be sure to set aside a tenth of all that your fields produce each year.

verses.text: You shall eat before Yahweh your God, in the place which he chooses, to cause his name to dwell there, the tithe of your grain, of your new wine, and of your oil, and the firstborn of your herd and of your flock; that you may learn to fear Yahweh your God always.
verses.explainer: Eat the tithe of your grain, new wine, and olive oil, and the firstborn of your herds and flocks in the presence of the Lord your God at the place he will choose as a dwelling for his Name, so that you may learn to revere the Lord your God always.

verses.text: If the way is too long for you, so that you are not able to carry it, because the place is too far from you, which Yahweh your God shall choose, to set his name there, when Yahweh your God shall bless you;
verses.explainer: But if the place the Lord your God chooses is too distant and you have been blessed by the Lord and cannot carry your tithe --

verses.text: then you shall turn it into money, and bind up the money in your hand, and shall go to the place which Yahweh your God shall choose.
verses.explainer: Then exchange your tithe for silver, take the silver with you, and go to the place the Lord will choose.

verses.text: You shall trade the money for whatever your soul desires, for cattle, or for sheep, or for wine, or for strong drink, or for whatever your soul asks of you; and you shall eat there before Yahweh your God, and you shall rejoice, you and your household.
verses.explainer: Use the silver to buy whatever you like: cattle, sheep, wine or other fermented drink, or anything you wish. Then you and your household shall eat there in the presence of the Lord your God and rejoice.

thenVsNow.then: The permission to convert tithes to money for long-distance travel and then buy 'whatever you wish' shows remarkable flexibility and trust. The emphasis is on joyful celebration in God's presence, not on the specific items consumed.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that generosity should be accompanied by joy challenges both the reluctant giver who resents the obligation and the ascetic who distrusts celebration.

verses.text: You shall not forsake the Levite who is within your gates, for he has no portion nor inheritance with you.
verses.explainer: And do not neglect the Levites living in your towns, for they have no allotment or inheritance of their own.

verses.text: At the end of every three years you shall bring all the tithe of your increase in the same year, and shall store it within your gates.
verses.explainer: At the end of every three years, bring all the tithes of that year's produce and store it in your towns.

verses.text: The Levite, because he has no portion nor inheritance with you, and the foreigner living among you, and the fatherless, and the widow, who are within your gates, shall come, and shall eat and be satisfied; that Yahweh your God may bless you in all the work of your hand which you do.
verses.explainer: So that the Levites (who have no allotment or inheritance of their own) and the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows who live in your towns may come and eat and be satisfied, and so that the Lord your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 26:12-13 -- The third-year tithe declaration confirms that the produce was distributed to Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows. Social welfare is built into the worship system.
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 26:12-13 -- The third-year tithe declaration confirms that the produce was distributed to Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows. Social welfare is built into the worship system.

explainer.summary: Every seventh year, debts are to be cancelled. Moses anticipates the objection: people will refuse to lend as the sabbatical year approaches. He commands generosity regardless, insisting that there should be no poor among God's people. Hebrew slaves are to be freed in the seventh year and sent away generously. If a slave chooses to stay, a ceremony of permanent attachment is performed with an awl through the ear.
themes: Debt cancellation every seven years
Generosity commanded despite economic risk
No poor should exist in God's community
Freedom for Hebrew slaves
Voluntary permanent servitude
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 extends the sabbatical principle from land (Leviticus 25) to debts and slavery. The economic legislation envisions a society where the cycle of poverty is regularly interrupted and where no one is permanently trapped in destitution.

wordStudies.significance: The 'shemittah' (release) occurs every seventh year. Debts are released, slaves are released, and the land rests. The seven-year rhythm applies the Sabbath principle to economics: just as humans need rest, economic systems need periodic reset to prevent permanent inequality.

verses.text: At the end of every seven years, you shall cancel debts.
verses.explainer: At the end of every seven years you must cancel debts.

verses.text: This is the way it shall be done: every creditor shall release that which he has lent to his neighbor. He shall not require payment from his neighbor and his brother; because Yahweh’s release has been proclaimed.
verses.explainer: This is how it is to be done: every creditor shall cancel any loan they have made to a fellow Israelite. They shall not require payment from anyone among their own people, because the Lord's time for canceling debts has been proclaimed.

verses.text: Of a foreigner you may require it; but whatever of yours is with your brother, your hand shall release.
verses.explainer: You may require payment from a foreigner, but you must cancel any debt your fellow Israelite owes you.

verses.text: However there shall be no poor with you (for Yahweh will surely bless you in the land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance to possess it)
verses.explainer: However, there need be no poor people among you, for in the land the Lord your God is giving you to possess as your inheritance, he will richly bless you.

thenVsNow.then: The bold declaration that there 'need be no poor' among God's people is aspirational: if Israel obeys fully, God's blessing will be so abundant that poverty will be eliminated.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been cited throughout history as the standard against which economic systems are measured. The New Testament echoes it in Acts 4:34: 'There were no needy persons among them.'

verses.text: if only you diligently listen to Yahweh your God’s voice, to observe to do all this commandment which I command you today.
verses.explainer: If only you fully obey the Lord your God and are careful to follow all these commands I am giving you today.

verses.text: For Yahweh your God will bless you, as he promised you. You will lend to many nations, but you will not borrow. You will rule over many nations, but they will not rule over you.
verses.explainer: For the Lord your God will bless you as he has promised, and you will lend to many nations but will borrow from none. You will rule over many nations but none will rule over you.

verses.text: If a poor man, one of your brothers, is with you within any of your gates in your land which Yahweh your God gives you, you shall not harden your heart, nor shut your hand from your poor brother;
verses.explainer: If anyone is poor among your fellow Israelites in any of the towns of the land the Lord is giving you, do not be hardhearted or tightfisted toward them.

verses.text: but you shall surely open your hand to him, and shall surely lend him sufficient for his need, which he lacks.
verses.explainer: Rather, be openhanded and freely lend them whatever they need.

verses.text: Beware that there not be a wicked thought in your heart, saying, “The seventh year, the year of release, is at hand”; and your eye be evil against your poor brother, and you give him nothing; and he cry to Yahweh against you, and it be sin to you.
verses.explainer: Be careful not to harbor this wicked thought: 'The seventh year, the year for canceling debts, is near,' so that you do not show ill will toward the needy among your fellow Israelites and give them nothing. They may then appeal to the Lord against you, and you will be found guilty of sin.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 6:12 -- Jesus' teaching on forgiveness of debts in the Lord's Prayer echoes the sabbatical principle: as God forgives our debts, we must forgive others'.
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 6:12 -- Jesus' teaching on forgiveness of debts in the Lord's Prayer echoes the sabbatical principle: as God forgives our debts, we must forgive others'.

verses.text: You shall surely give, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give to him; because that for this thing Yahweh your God will bless you in all your work, and in all that you put your hand to.
verses.explainer: Give generously to them and do so without a grudging heart; then because of this the Lord your God will bless you in all your work and in everything you put your hand to.

verses.text: For the poor will never cease out of the land. Therefore I command you to surely open your hand to your brother, to your needy, and to your poor, in your land.
verses.explainer: There will always be poor people in the land. Therefore I command you to be openhanded toward your fellow Israelites who are poor and needy in your land.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 26:11 -- Jesus quotes this verse: 'The poor you will always have with you.' He is not dismissing poverty but citing Moses' argument that perpetual need requires perpetual generosity.
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 26:11 -- Jesus quotes this verse: 'The poor you will always have with you.' He is not dismissing poverty but citing Moses' argument that perpetual need requires perpetual generosity.

verses.text: If your brother, a Hebrew man, or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you, and serves you six years; then in the seventh year you shall let him go free from you.
verses.explainer: If any of your people -- Hebrew men or women -- sell themselves to you and serve you six years, in the seventh year you must let them go free.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Hebrew slavery was debt-bondage, not chattel slavery. The person worked to pay off a debt. The six-year limit ensured that economic hardship did not become permanent servitude.

verses.text: When you let him go free from you, you shall not let him go empty.
verses.explainer: And when you release them, do not send them away empty-handed.

verses.text: You shall furnish him liberally out of your flock, out of your threshing floor, and out of your wine press. As Yahweh your God has blessed you, you shall give to him.
verses.explainer: Supply them liberally from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress. Give to them as the Lord your God has blessed you.

thenVsNow.then: Freed slaves were to be equipped for economic independence -- not merely released but resourced. The generosity mirrors God's generosity in releasing Israel from Egypt.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that liberation requires resources, not just freedom, has implications for any system of rehabilitation or release. Freedom without means to sustain it is incomplete.

verses.text: You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and Yahweh your God redeemed you. Therefore I command you this thing today.
verses.explainer: Remember that you were slaves in Egypt and the Lord your God redeemed you. That is why I give you this command today.

verses.text: It shall be, if he tells you, “I will not go out from you,” because he loves you and your house, because he is well with you;
verses.explainer: But if your servant says to you, 'I do not want to leave you,' because he loves you and your family and is well off with you --

verses.text: then you shall take an awl, and thrust it through his ear to the door, and he shall be your servant forever. Also to your female servant you shall do likewise.
verses.explainer: Then take an awl and push it through his earlobe into the door, and he will become your servant for life. Do the same for your female servant.

crossReferences.reference: Psalm 40:6 -- 'My ears you have opened' (literally 'dug') may allude to this ceremony, with the psalmist expressing voluntary dedication to God's service.
crossReferences.connection: Psalm 40:6 -- 'My ears you have opened' (literally 'dug') may allude to this ceremony, with the psalmist expressing voluntary dedication to God's service.

verses.text: It shall not seem hard to you, when you let him go free from you; for he has been double value of a hired hand as he served you six years. Yahweh your God will bless you in all that you do.
verses.explainer: Do not consider it a hardship to set your servant free, because their service to you these six years has been worth twice as much as that of a hired hand. And the Lord your God will bless you in everything you do.

verses.text: You shall dedicate all the firstborn males that are born of your herd and of your flock to Yahweh your God. You shall do no work with the firstborn of your herd, nor shear the firstborn of your flock.
verses.explainer: Set apart for the Lord your God every firstborn male of your herds and flocks. Do not put the firstborn of your cows to work, and do not shear the firstborn of your sheep.

verses.text: You shall eat it before Yahweh your God year by year in the place which Yahweh shall choose, you and your household.
verses.explainer: Each year you and your family are to eat them in the presence of the Lord your God at the place he will choose.

verses.text: If it has any defect, is lame or blind, or has any defect whatever, you shall not sacrifice it to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: If an animal has a defect, is lame or blind, or has any serious flaw, you must not sacrifice it to the Lord your God.

verses.text: You shall eat it within your gates. The unclean and the clean shall eat it alike, as the gazelle, and as the deer.
verses.explainer: You are to eat it in your own towns. Both the ceremonially unclean and the clean may eat it, as if it were gazelle or deer.

verses.text: Only you shall not eat its blood. You shall pour it out on the ground like water.
verses.explainer: But you must not eat the blood; pour it out on the ground like water.

explainer.summary: Moses prescribes the three annual pilgrimage festivals: Passover/Unleavened Bread, the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot), and the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot). All males must appear before the Lord at the central sanctuary three times a year. The chapter concludes with regulations for appointing judges and the prohibition of bribery. The connection between festivals and justice is intentional: worship and ethics belong together.
themes: Three annual pilgrimage festivals
Passover as the foundation event
Feast of Weeks -- harvest gratitude
Feast of Tabernacles -- the greatest celebration
Justice and impartial judges
explainer.contextInBook: The festivals connect worship (chapters 12-13) with daily ethics (chapters 14-15). By requiring three annual pilgrimages, the law ensures that Israelites regularly leave their local communities and gather as a unified nation before God.

wordStudies.significance: The three pilgrimage festivals (shalosh regalim) required all males to travel to the central sanctuary. 'Chag' comes from a root meaning 'to circle' or 'to dance,' suggesting celebration and movement. These were not solemn observances but joyful national gatherings.

verses.text: Observe the month of Abib, and keep the Passover to Yahweh your God; for in the month of Abib Yahweh your God brought you out of Egypt by night.
verses.explainer: Observe the month of Aviv and celebrate the Passover of the Lord your God, because in the month of Aviv he brought you out of Egypt by night.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 12:1-14 -- The original Passover instructions. Deuteronomy does not repeat the full ritual but emphasizes that Passover must now be observed at the central sanctuary.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 12:1-14 -- The original Passover instructions. Deuteronomy does not repeat the full ritual but emphasizes that Passover must now be observed at the central sanctuary.

verses.text: You shall sacrifice the Passover to Yahweh your God, of the flock and the herd, in the place which Yahweh shall choose, to cause his name to dwell there.
verses.explainer: Sacrifice as the Passover to the Lord your God an animal from your flock or herd at the place the Lord will choose as a dwelling for his Name.

verses.text: You shall eat no leavened bread with it. You shall eat unleavened bread with it seven days, even the bread of affliction; for you came out of the land of Egypt in haste; that you may remember the day when you came out of the land of Egypt all the days of your life.
verses.explainer: Do not eat it with bread made with yeast, but for seven days eat unleavened bread, the bread of affliction, because you left Egypt in haste -- so that all the days of your life you may remember the time of your departure from Egypt.

verses.text: No yeast shall be seen with you in all your borders seven days; neither shall any of the meat, which you sacrifice the first day at evening, remain all night until the morning.
verses.explainer: Let no yeast be found in your possession in all your land for seven days. Do not let any of the meat you sacrifice on the evening of the first day remain until morning.

verses.text: You may not sacrifice the Passover within any of your gates, which Yahweh your God gives you;
verses.explainer: You must not sacrifice the Passover in any town the Lord gives you.

verses.text: but at the place which Yahweh your God shall choose, to cause his name to dwell in, there you shall sacrifice the Passover at evening, at the going down of the sun, at the season that you came out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Except in the place he will choose as a dwelling for his Name. There you must sacrifice the Passover in the evening, when the sun goes down, on the anniversary of your departure from Egypt.

verses.text: You shall roast and eat it in the place which Yahweh your God chooses. In the morning you shall return to your tents.
verses.explainer: Roast it and eat it at the place the Lord will choose. Then in the morning return to your tents.

verses.text: Six days you shall eat unleavened bread. On the seventh day shall be a solemn assembly to Yahweh your God. You shall do no work.
verses.explainer: For six days eat unleavened bread. On the seventh day hold an assembly to the Lord your God and do no work.

verses.text: You shall count for yourselves seven weeks. From the time you begin to put the sickle to the standing grain you shall begin to count seven weeks.
verses.explainer: Count off seven weeks from the time you begin to put the sickle to the standing grain.

thenVsNow.then: The Feast of Weeks was an agricultural festival marking the wheat harvest, seven weeks after the barley harvest began at Passover.
thenVsNow.now: Christians identify Pentecost (the Greek name for this festival) with the outpouring of the Holy Spirit in Acts 2. The harvest imagery shifts from grain to souls.

verses.text: You shall keep the feast of weeks to Yahweh your God with a tribute of a freewill offering of your hand, which you shall give, according as Yahweh your God blesses you.
verses.explainer: Then celebrate the Festival of Weeks to the Lord your God by giving a freewill offering in proportion to the blessings the Lord your God has given you.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The offering is proportional to blessing -- those who received more give more. This principle of proportional generosity appears throughout Deuteronomy.

verses.text: You shall rejoice before Yahweh your God: you, your son, your daughter, your male servant, your female servant, the Levite who is within your gates, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, who are among you, in the place which Yahweh your God shall choose to cause his name to dwell there.
verses.explainer: And rejoice before the Lord your God -- you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, the Levites in your towns, and the foreigners, the fatherless, and the widows living among you.

verses.text: You shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt. You shall observe and do these statutes.
verses.explainer: Remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and follow carefully these decrees.

verses.text: You shall keep the feast of tents seven days, after you have gathered in from your threshing floor and from your wine press.
verses.explainer: Celebrate the Festival of Tabernacles for seven days after you have gathered the produce of your threshing floor and your winepress.

verses.text: You shall rejoice in your feast, you, your son, your daughter, your male servant, your female servant, the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, who are within your gates.
verses.explainer: Be joyful at your festival -- you, your sons and daughters, your male and female servants, and the Levites, the foreigners, the fatherless and the widows who live in your towns.

verses.text: You shall keep a feast to Yahweh your God seven days in the place which Yahweh chooses; because Yahweh your God will bless you in all your increase, and in all the work of your hands, and you shall be altogether joyful.
verses.explainer: For seven days celebrate the festival to the Lord your God at the place the Lord will choose. For the Lord will bless you in all your harvest and in all the work of your hands, and your joy will be complete.

verses.text: Three times in a year all of your males shall appear before Yahweh your God in the place which he chooses: in the feast of unleavened bread, in the feast of weeks, and in the feast of tents. They shall not appear before Yahweh empty.
verses.explainer: Three times a year all your men must appear before the Lord your God at the place he will choose: at the Festival of Unleavened Bread, the Festival of Weeks, and the Festival of Tabernacles. No one should appear before the Lord empty-handed.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 23:14-17 -- The three pilgrimage festivals were first prescribed in the Book of the Covenant. Deuteronomy restates them with the emphasis on the central sanctuary.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 23:14-17 -- The three pilgrimage festivals were first prescribed in the Book of the Covenant. Deuteronomy restates them with the emphasis on the central sanctuary.

verses.text: Every man shall give as he is able, according to Yahweh your God’s blessing which he has given you.
verses.explainer: Each of you must bring a gift in proportion to the way the Lord your God has blessed you.

verses.text: You shall make judges and officers in all your gates, which Yahweh your God gives you, according to your tribes; and they shall judge the people with righteous judgment.
verses.explainer: Appoint judges and officials for each of your tribes in every town the Lord your God is giving you, and they shall judge the people fairly.

thenVsNow.then: The transition from festivals to judges is deliberate: a society that worships correctly must also govern justly. Worship without justice is empty ritual.
thenVsNow.now: The connection between worship and justice challenges any faith tradition that separates personal devotion from public ethics. They are inseparable in Deuteronomy.

verses.text: You shall not pervert justice. You shall not show partiality. You shall not take a bribe, for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise, and perverts the words of the righteous.
verses.explainer: Do not pervert justice or show partiality. Do not accept a bribe, for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and twists the words of the innocent.

verses.text: You shall follow that which is altogether just, that you may live, and inherit the land which Yahweh your God gives you.
verses.explainer: Follow justice and justice alone, so that you may live and possess the land the Lord your God is giving you.

verses.text: You shall not plant for yourselves an Asherah of any kind of tree beside Yahweh your God’s altar, which you shall make for yourselves.
verses.explainer: Do not set up any wooden Asherah pole beside the altar you build to the Lord your God.

verses.text: Neither shall you set yourself up a sacred stone which Yahweh your God hates.
verses.explainer: And do not erect a sacred stone, for these the Lord your God hates.

explainer.summary: Moses establishes a court system with a supreme court at the central sanctuary for difficult cases. He then provides the law of the king -- if Israel demands a king, he must be chosen by God, must be an Israelite, and must not accumulate excessive horses, wives, or wealth. The king must write his own copy of the law and read it daily. This 'constitutional monarchy' limits royal power in ways unprecedented in the ancient Near East.
themes: Due process and the supreme court
Two-witness requirement for capital cases
The law of the king -- limited monarchy
The king under the law, not above it
Royal humility as constitutional requirement
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 17 moves from local judges (16:18-20) to the supreme court and then to the monarchy. The progression is logical: every legal system needs an appellate authority, and every government needs limits on power.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Copy of this law / second law
wordStudies.significance: The king must write a 'mishneh' (copy) of the law. The word 'mishneh' also means 'second' or 'repetition,' which is related to the book's Greek title 'Deuteronomy' (second law). The king's personal copy ensured he could not claim ignorance of the law he was bound to obey.

verses.text: You shall not sacrifice to Yahweh your God an ox, or a sheep, in which is a defect, or anything evil; for that is an abomination to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Do not sacrifice to the Lord your God an ox or a sheep that has any defect or flaw in it, for that would be detestable to him.

verses.text: If there is found among you, within any of your gates which Yahweh your God gives you, a man or woman who does that which is evil in Yahweh your God’s sight, in transgressing his covenant,
verses.explainer: If a man or woman living among you is found doing evil in the eyes of the Lord by violating his covenant --

verses.text: and has gone and served other gods, and worshiped them, or the sun, or the moon, or any of the stars of the sky, which I have not commanded;
verses.explainer: Worshiping other gods, bowing down to them or to the sun or moon or stars of the sky, which I have forbidden --

verses.text: and you are told, and you have heard of it, then you shall inquire diligently. Behold, if it is true, and the thing certain, that such abomination is done in Israel,
verses.explainer: And this has been brought to your attention, then you must investigate it thoroughly. If it is true and it has been proved that this detestable thing has been done in Israel --

verses.text: then you shall bring out that man or that woman, who has done this evil thing, to your gates, even that same man or woman; and you shall stone them to death with stones.
verses.explainer: Take the man or woman who has done this evil deed to your city gate and stone that person to death.

verses.text: At the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, he who is to die shall be put to death. At the mouth of one witness he shall not be put to death.
verses.explainer: On the testimony of two or three witnesses a person is to be put to death, but no one is to be put to death on the testimony of only one witness.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 18:16 -- Jesus incorporates the two-witness principle into church discipline: 'Take one or two others along, so that every matter may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 18:16 -- Jesus incorporates the two-witness principle into church discipline: 'Take one or two others along, so that every matter may be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.'

verses.text: The hand of the witnesses shall be first on him to put him to death, and afterward the hand of all the people. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: The hands of the witnesses must be the first in putting that person to death, and then the hands of all the people. You must purge the evil from among you.

verses.text: If there arises a matter too hard for you in judgment, between blood and blood, between plea and plea, and between stroke and stroke, being matters of controversy within your gates; then you shall arise, and go up to the place which Yahweh your God chooses.
verses.explainer: If cases come before your courts that are too difficult for you to judge -- disputes about bloodshed, lawsuits, or assaults -- take them to the place the Lord will choose.

verses.text: You shall come to the priests who are Levites, and to the judge who shall be in those days. You shall inquire, and they shall give you the verdict.
verses.explainer: Go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is in office at that time. Inquire of them and they will give you the verdict.

verses.text: You shall do according to the decisions of the verdict which they shall give you from that place which Yahweh chooses. You shall observe to do according to all that they shall teach you:
verses.explainer: You must act according to the decisions they give you at the place the Lord will choose. Be careful to do everything they instruct you to do.

verses.text: according to the decisions of the law which they shall teach you, and according to the judgment which they shall tell you, you shall do. You shall not turn away from the sentence which they shall show you, to the right hand, nor to the left.
verses.explainer: Act according to whatever they teach you and the decisions they give you. Do not turn aside from what they tell you, to the right or to the left.

verses.text: The man who does presumptuously, in not listening to the priest who stands to minister there before Yahweh your God, or to the judge, even that man shall die. You shall put away the evil from Israel.
verses.explainer: Anyone who shows contempt for the judge or for the priest who stands ministering there to the Lord your God is to be put to death. You must purge the evil from Israel.

verses.text: All the people shall hear, and fear, and do no more presumptuously.
verses.explainer: All the people will hear and be afraid, and will not be contemptuous again.

verses.text: When you have come to the land which Yahweh your God gives you, and possess it, and dwell in it, and say, “I will set a king over me, like all the nations that are around me”;
verses.explainer: When you enter the land the Lord your God is giving you and have taken possession of it and settled in it, and you say, 'Let us set a king over us like all the nations around us' --

verses.text: you shall surely set him king over yourselves, whom Yahweh your God chooses. You shall set as king over you one from among your brothers. You may not put a foreigner over you, who is not your brother.
verses.explainer: Be sure to appoint over you a king the Lord your God chooses. He must be from among your fellow Israelites. Do not place a foreigner over you, one who is not an Israelite.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Samuel 9:15-17 -- God chooses Saul as Israel's first king, fulfilling this provision. Later, he chooses David (1 Samuel 16:1-13).
crossReferences.connection: 1 Samuel 9:15-17 -- God chooses Saul as Israel's first king, fulfilling this provision. Later, he chooses David (1 Samuel 16:1-13).

verses.text: Only he shall not multiply horses to himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt, to the end that he may multiply horses; because Yahweh has said to you, “You shall not go back that way again.”
verses.explainer: The king, moreover, must not acquire great numbers of horses for himself or make the people return to Egypt to get more of them, for the Lord has told you, 'You are not to go back that way again.'

thenVsNow.then: Horses represented military power (chariots) and political alliances (Egypt was the horse supermarket). Limiting horses prevented military adventurism and dependence on foreign alliances.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that leaders should not accumulate instruments of power beyond what is needed remains relevant. Excessive military buildup can become its own temptation.

verses.text: He shall not multiply wives to himself, that his heart not turn away. He shall not greatly multiply to himself silver and gold.
verses.explainer: He must not take many wives, or his heart will be led astray. He must not accumulate large amounts of silver and gold.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Kings 11:1-4 -- Solomon violated every provision of this verse: many horses, many wives, and enormous wealth. His heart was indeed led astray, exactly as Moses predicted.
crossReferences.connection: 1 Kings 11:1-4 -- Solomon violated every provision of this verse: many horses, many wives, and enormous wealth. His heart was indeed led astray, exactly as Moses predicted.

verses.text: It shall be, when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, that he shall write himself a copy of this law in a book, out of that which is before the priests the Levites.
verses.explainer: When he takes the throne of his kingdom, he is to write for himself on a scroll a copy of this law, taken from that of the Levitical priests.

verses.text: It shall be with him, and he shall read from it all the days of his life; that he may learn to fear Yahweh his God, to keep all the words of this law and these statutes, to do them;
verses.explainer: It is to be with him, and he is to read it all the days of his life so that he may learn to revere the Lord his God and follow carefully all the words of this law and these decrees.

verses.text: that his heart not be lifted up above his brothers, and that he not turn away from the commandment, to the right hand, or to the left; to the end that he may prolong his days in his kingdom, he and his children, in the middle of Israel.
verses.explainer: He is not to consider himself better than his fellow Israelites and turn from the law to the right or to the left. Then he and his descendants will reign a long time over his kingdom in Israel.

explainer.summary: Moses provides for the Levitical priests' support since they have no land inheritance. He then prohibits Canaanite divination practices (sorcery, fortune-telling, consulting the dead) and promises that God will raise up a prophet like Moses from among the Israelites. This prophet will speak God's words, and the people must listen to him. The chapter concludes with a test for prophets: if a prediction does not come true, that prophet has spoken presumptuously.
themes: Levitical priests supported by the community
Prohibition of all forms of divination
The promise of a prophet like Moses
How to identify false prophets
God's word through prophets, not magic
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 18 completes the institutional framework: judges (16:18-17:13), kings (17:14-20), priests (18:1-8), and prophets (18:9-22). Each institution has defined authority and limits. Together they form a system of distributed power that prevents any single leader from becoming absolute.

wordStudies.significance: The 'navi' speaks on behalf of God. The word may come from a root meaning 'to call' or 'to bubble forth.' The prophet is not a fortune-teller but a covenant enforcer -- calling Israel back to faithfulness through God's authoritative word.

verses.text: The priests the Levites, all the tribe of Levi, shall have no portion nor inheritance with Israel. They shall eat the offerings of Yahweh made by fire and his portion.
verses.explainer: The Levitical priests -- indeed the whole tribe of Levi -- are to have no allotment or inheritance with Israel. They shall live on the food offerings presented to the Lord, for that is their inheritance.

verses.text: They shall have no inheritance among their brothers. Yahweh is their inheritance, as he has spoken to them.
verses.explainer: They shall have no inheritance among their fellow Israelites; the Lord is their inheritance, as he promised them.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Levites' lack of land inheritance made them dependent on the community's faithfulness in tithing and offering. When Israel's worship declined, the Levites suffered first -- making them a barometer of national spiritual health.

verses.text: This shall be the priests’ due from the people, from those who offer a sacrifice, whether it be ox or sheep, that they shall give to the priest the shoulder, the two cheeks, and the inner parts.
verses.explainer: This is the share due the priests from the people who sacrifice a bull or a sheep: the shoulder, the internal organs, and the meat from the head.

verses.text: The first fruits of your grain, of your new wine, and of your oil, and the first of the fleece of your sheep, you shall give him.
verses.explainer: You are to give them the firstfruits of your grain, new wine, and olive oil, and the first wool from the shearing of your sheep.

verses.text: For Yahweh your God has chosen him out of all your tribes, to stand to minister in Yahweh’s name, him and his sons for ever.
verses.explainer: For the Lord your God has chosen them and their descendants out of all your tribes to stand and minister in the Lord's name always.

verses.text: If a Levite comes from any of your gates out of all Israel, where he lives as a foreigner, and comes with all the desire of his soul to the place which Yahweh shall choose;
verses.explainer: If a Levite moves from one of your towns anywhere in Israel where he is living, and comes in all earnestness to the place the Lord will choose --

verses.text: then he shall minister in the name of Yahweh his God, as all his brothers the Levites do, who stand there before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: He may minister in the name of the Lord his God like all his fellow Levites who serve there in the presence of the Lord.

verses.text: They shall have like portions to eat, in addition to that which comes from the sale of his family possessions.
verses.explainer: He is to share equally in their benefits, even though he has received money from the sale of family possessions.

verses.text: When you have come into the land which Yahweh your God gives you, you shall not learn to imitate the abominations of those nations.
verses.explainer: When you enter the land the Lord your God is giving you, do not learn to imitate the detestable ways of the nations there.

verses.text: There shall not be found with you anyone who makes his son or his daughter to pass through the fire, one who uses divination, one who practices sorcery, or an enchanter, or a sorcerer,
verses.explainer: Let no one be found among you who sacrifices their son or daughter in the fire, who practices divination or sorcery, interprets omens, engages in witchcraft.

verses.text: or a charmer, or a consulter with a familiar spirit, or a wizard, or a necromancer.
verses.explainer: Or casts spells, or who is a medium or spiritist or who consults the dead.

verses.text: For whoever does these things is an abomination to Yahweh. Because of these abominations, Yahweh your God drives them out from before you.
verses.explainer: Anyone who does these things is detestable to the Lord; because of these same detestable practices the Lord your God will drive out those nations before you.

thenVsNow.then: These practices were not merely superstitious but constituted alternative systems of knowledge that bypassed God's revealed word. They sought power and information outside the covenant relationship.
thenVsNow.now: The underlying principle is that seeking supernatural knowledge or power outside of God's authorized channels is prohibited. The specific forms change across cultures, but the prohibition against unauthorized spiritual manipulation endures.

verses.text: You shall be perfect with Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: You must be blameless before the Lord your God.

verses.text: For these nations that you shall dispossess listen to those who practice sorcery and to diviners; but as for you, Yahweh your God has not allowed you so to do.
verses.explainer: The nations you will dispossess listen to those who practice sorcery or divination. But as for you, the Lord your God has not permitted you to do so.

verses.text: Yahweh your God will raise up to you a prophet from among you, of your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him.
verses.explainer: The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your fellow Israelites. You must listen to him.

crossReferences.reference: Acts 3:22-23 -- Peter identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of this prophecy: 'Moses said, The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people.'
crossReferences.connection: Acts 3:22-23 -- Peter identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of this prophecy: 'Moses said, The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your own people.'

crossReferences.reference: John 1:21 -- The Jews ask John the Baptist, 'Are you the Prophet?' -- referring to this specific Deuteronomy promise.
crossReferences.connection: John 1:21 -- The Jews ask John the Baptist, 'Are you the Prophet?' -- referring to this specific Deuteronomy promise.

verses.text: This is according to all that you desired of Yahweh your God in Horeb in the day of the assembly, saying, “Let me not hear again Yahweh my God’s voice, neither let me see this great fire any more, that I not die.”
verses.explainer: For this is what you asked of the Lord your God at Horeb on the day of the assembly when you said, 'Let us not hear the voice of the Lord our God nor see this great fire anymore, or we will die.'

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “They have well said that which they have spoken.
verses.explainer: The Lord said to me: 'What they say is good.'

verses.text: I will raise them up a prophet from among their brothers, like you. I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I shall command him.
verses.explainer: I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites, and I will put my words in his mouth. He will tell them everything I command him.

crossReferences.reference: John 12:49 -- Jesus says: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken.' He fulfills this verse precisely.
crossReferences.connection: John 12:49 -- Jesus says: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken.' He fulfills this verse precisely.

verses.text: It shall happen, that whoever will not listen to my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it of him.
verses.explainer: I myself will call to account anyone who does not listen to my words that the prophet speaks in my name.

verses.text: But the prophet who speaks a word presumptuously in my name, which I have not commanded him to speak, or who speaks in the name of other gods, that same prophet shall die.”
verses.explainer: But a prophet who presumes to speak in my name anything I have not commanded, or a prophet who speaks in the name of other gods, is to be put to death.

verses.text: You may say in your heart, “How shall we know the word which Yahweh has not spoken?”
verses.explainer: You may say to yourselves, 'How can we know when a message has not been spoken by the Lord?'

verses.text: When a prophet speaks in Yahweh’s name, if the thing doesn’t follow, nor happen, that is the thing which Yahweh has not spoken. The prophet has spoken it presumptuously. You shall not be afraid of him.
verses.explainer: If what a prophet proclaims in the name of the Lord does not take place or come true, that is a message the Lord has not spoken. That prophet has spoken presumptuously, so do not be alarmed.

thenVsNow.then: The test of a prophet is whether their predictions come true. This is a practical, verifiable criterion that prevents anyone from claiming prophetic authority without accountability.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of testing prophetic claims against verifiable outcomes remains essential. Claims of divine authority require evidence, not just charisma.

explainer.summary: Moses expands on the cities of refuge, explaining that three cities must be established in Canaan (with the possibility of three more if the territory expands). He provides a concrete example of accidental killing -- a man whose axe head flies off while chopping wood. He also addresses the sanctity of property boundaries and establishes the law against false witnesses: a false accuser receives the punishment they intended for their victim.
themes: Cities of refuge for accidental killers
Roads maintained for quick access to refuge
Boundary stones as sacred markers
False witnesses punished with intended victim's penalty
Justice that protects and punishes
explainer.contextInBook: After establishing the judicial system (16-17) and the prophetic office (18), Moses addresses specific justice issues: protecting the innocent from blood vengeance, protecting property boundaries, and protecting the accused from false testimony.

wordStudies.significance: The principle 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth' (v.21) is not a license for revenge but a limitation on punishment: the punishment must be proportional to the crime, no more, no less. It prevents both excessive vengeance and inadequate justice.

verses.text: When Yahweh your God cuts off the nations whose land Yahweh your God gives you, and you succeed them and dwell in their cities and in their houses;
verses.explainer: When the Lord your God has destroyed the nations whose land he is giving you, and when you have driven them out and settled in their towns and houses --

verses.text: you shall set apart three cities for yourselves in the middle of your land, which Yahweh your God gives you to possess it.
verses.explainer: Then set apart three cities for yourselves in the land the Lord your God is giving you to possess.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 35:14 -- Together with the three cities already designated east of the Jordan (Deuteronomy 4:41-43), these bring the total to six cities of refuge.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 35:14 -- Together with the three cities already designated east of the Jordan (Deuteronomy 4:41-43), these bring the total to six cities of refuge.

verses.text: You shall prepare the way, and divide the borders of your land which Yahweh your God causes you to inherit into three parts, that every man slayer may flee there.
verses.explainer: Determine the distances involved and divide into three parts the land the Lord is giving you as an inheritance, so that a person who kills someone may flee for refuge to one of these cities.

verses.text: This is the case of the man slayer who shall flee there and live. Whoever kills his neighbor unawares, and didn’t hate him in time past;
verses.explainer: This is the rule concerning anyone who kills a person and flees there for safety -- anyone who kills a neighbor unintentionally, without malice aforethought.

verses.text: as when a man goes into the forest with his neighbor to chop wood and his hand swings the ax to cut down the tree, and the head slips from the handle and hits his neighbor so that he dies, he shall flee to one of these cities and live.
verses.explainer: For instance, a man may go into the forest with his neighbor to cut wood, and as he swings his ax to fell a tree, the head may fly off and hit his neighbor and kill him. That man may flee to one of these cities and save his life.

thenVsNow.then: The axe-head example is strikingly specific and practical. It describes an everyday work accident that could easily result in death. The law anticipates real situations, not abstract principles.
thenVsNow.now: Modern legal systems distinguish between intentional and accidental killing (murder vs. manslaughter) following principles first articulated in passages like this one.

verses.text: Otherwise, the avenger of blood might pursue the man slayer while his heart is hot and overtake him, because the way is long, and strike him mortally; even though he was not worthy of death, because he didn’t hate him in time past.
verses.explainer: Otherwise, the avenger of blood might pursue him in a rage, overtake him if the distance is too great, and kill him even though he is not deserving of death, since he did it to his neighbor without malice aforethought.

verses.text: Therefore I command you to set apart three cities for yourselves.
verses.explainer: This is why I command you to set aside for yourselves three cities.

verses.text: If Yahweh your God enlarges your border, as he has sworn to your fathers, and gives you all the land which he promised to give to your fathers;
verses.explainer: If the Lord your God enlarges your territory, as he promised your ancestors, and gives you the whole land he promised them --

verses.text: if you keep all this commandment to do it, which I command you today, to love Yahweh your God, and to walk ever in his ways; then you shall add three cities more for yourselves, besides these three.
verses.explainer: Because you carefully follow all these laws I command you today -- to love the Lord your God and to walk always in obedience to him -- then you are to set aside three more cities.

verses.text: This is so that innocent blood will not be shed in the middle of your land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance, leaving blood guilt on you.
verses.explainer: Do this so that innocent blood will not be shed in your land, which the Lord your God is giving you as your inheritance, and so that you will not be guilty of bloodshed.

verses.text: But if any man hates his neighbor, lies in wait for him, rises up against him, strikes him mortally so that he dies, and he flees into one of these cities;
verses.explainer: But if out of hate someone lies in wait, assaults and kills a neighbor, and then flees to one of these cities --

verses.text: then the elders of his city shall send and bring him there, and deliver him into the hand of the avenger of blood, that he may die.
verses.explainer: The killer shall be sent for by the town elders, be brought back from the city, and be handed over to the avenger of blood to die.

verses.text: Your eye shall not pity him, but you shall purge the innocent blood from Israel that it may go well with you.
verses.explainer: Show the killer no pity. You must purge from Israel the guilt of shedding innocent blood, so that it may go well with you.

verses.text: You shall not remove your neighbor’s landmark, which they of old time have set, in your inheritance which you shall inherit, in the land that Yahweh your God gives you to possess.
verses.explainer: Do not move your neighbor's boundary stone set up by your predecessors in the inheritance you receive in the land the Lord your God is giving you to possess.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Boundary stones (termini) were sacred in the ancient Near East. Moving them was considered a crime against both the neighbor and God. The land distribution was divinely ordered; altering boundaries violated God's arrangement.

verses.text: One witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin, in any sin that he sins. At the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall a matter be established.
verses.explainer: One witness is not enough to convict anyone accused of any crime or offense they may have committed. A matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.

crossReferences.reference: 2 Corinthians 13:1 -- Paul applies this principle to church discipline: 'Every matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.'
crossReferences.connection: 2 Corinthians 13:1 -- Paul applies this principle to church discipline: 'Every matter must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses.'

verses.text: If an unrighteous witness rises up against any man to testify against him of wrongdoing,
verses.explainer: If a malicious witness takes the stand to accuse someone of a crime --

verses.text: then both the men, between whom the controversy is, shall stand before Yahweh, before the priests and the judges who shall be in those days;
verses.explainer: The two parties to the dispute must stand in the presence of the Lord before the priests and the judges who are in office at the time.

verses.text: and the judges shall make diligent inquisition: and behold, if the witness is a false witness, and has testified falsely against his brother;
verses.explainer: The judges must make a thorough investigation, and if the witness proves to be a liar, giving false testimony --

verses.text: then you shall do to him as he had thought to do to his brother. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: Then do to the false witness as that witness intended to do to the other party. You must purge the evil from among you.

verses.text: Those who remain shall hear, and fear, and will never again commit any such evil among you.
verses.explainer: The rest of the people will hear of this and be afraid, and never again will such an evil thing be done among you.

verses.text: Your eyes shall not pity: life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.
verses.explainer: Show no pity: life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 5:38-39 -- Jesus cites and reinterprets this principle: 'You have heard that it was said, Eye for eye, and tooth for tooth. But I tell you, do not resist an evil person.'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 5:38-39 -- Jesus cites and reinterprets this principle: 'You have heard that it was said, Eye for eye, and tooth for tooth. But I tell you, do not resist an evil person.'

explainer.summary: Moses establishes Israel's rules of engagement for warfare. Before battle, a priest encourages the troops and announces exemptions: men who have built a new house, planted a new vineyard, become engaged, or are afraid may go home.
