Cities outside Canaan must first be offered peace terms; if they accept, the inhabitants become forced laborers. If they resist, the men are killed. Cities within Canaan receive no peace offer -- they are to be completely destroyed. Fruit trees may not be cut down during a siege.
themes: Faith in battle -- do not be afraid
Exemptions from military service
Peace terms offered to distant cities
Total destruction of Canaanite cities
Protection of fruit trees during siege
explainer.contextInBook: Warfare regulations follow the justice chapter (19) because war is the extreme case of justice between nations. Even in war, Deuteronomy insists on limits and procedures. The chapter distinguishes between wars of conquest (within Canaan) and wars of defense/expansion (outside Canaan).

wordStudies.significance: Before besieging distant cities, Israel must offer 'shalom' -- peace terms. The word encompasses more than cease-fire; it implies a settled relationship. Even in the context of war, peace is presented as the preferred outcome.

verses.text: When you go out to battle against your enemies, and see horses, chariots, and a people more than you, you shall not be afraid of them; for Yahweh your God is with you, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: When you go to war against your enemies and see horses and chariots and an army greater than yours, do not be afraid of them, because the Lord your God, who brought you up out of Egypt, will be with you.

verses.text: It shall be, when you draw near to the battle, that the priest shall approach and speak to the people,
verses.explainer: When you are about to go into battle, the priest shall come forward and address the army.

verses.text: and shall tell them, “Hear, Israel, you draw near today to battle against your enemies. Don’t let your heart faint! Don’t be afraid, nor tremble, neither be scared of them;
verses.explainer: He shall say: 'Hear, Israel: Today you are going into battle against your enemies. Do not be fainthearted or afraid; do not panic or be terrified by them.'

verses.text: for Yahweh your God is he who goes with you, to fight for you against your enemies, to save you.”
verses.explainer: For the Lord your God is the one who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies to give you victory.

verses.text: The officers shall speak to the people, saying, “What man is there who has built a new house, and has not dedicated it? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man dedicate it.
verses.explainer: The officers shall say to the army: 'Has anyone built a new house and not yet begun to live in it? Let him go home, or he may die in battle and someone else may begin to live in it.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The military exemptions are remarkable in the ancient world: new homeowners, new farmers, newly engaged men, and the afraid are all excused. Israel's army was to be composed only of willing, settled warriors whose affairs were in order.

verses.text: What man is there who has planted a vineyard, and has not used its fruit? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man use its fruit.
verses.explainer: Has anyone planted a vineyard and not begun to enjoy it? Let him go home, or he may die in battle and someone else enjoy it.

verses.text: What man is there who has pledged to be married a wife, and has not taken her? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle, and another man take her.”
verses.explainer: Has anyone become pledged to a woman and not married her? Let him go home, or he may die in battle and someone else marry her.

thenVsNow.then: These exemptions ensured that no soldier fought with unfinished business weighing on his mind. A distracted warrior is a danger to himself and others.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that personal readiness affects public service applies beyond warfare. People burdened with unresolved personal matters cannot fully engage in communal responsibilities.

verses.text: The officers shall speak further to the people, and they shall say, “What man is there who is fearful and faint-hearted? Let him go and return to his house, lest his brother’s heart melt as his heart.”
verses.explainer: Then the officers shall add: 'Is anyone afraid or fainthearted? Let him go home so that his fellow soldiers will not become disheartened too.'

crossReferences.reference: Judges 7:3 -- God uses this same principle to reduce Gideon's army from 32,000 to 10,000 and then to 300, ensuring that victory could only be attributed to God.
crossReferences.connection: Judges 7:3 -- God uses this same principle to reduce Gideon's army from 32,000 to 10,000 and then to 300, ensuring that victory could only be attributed to God.

verses.text: It shall be, when the officers have finished speaking to the people, that they shall appoint captains of armies at the head of the people.
verses.explainer: When the officers have finished speaking to the army, they shall appoint commanders over it.

verses.text: When you draw near to a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace to it.
verses.explainer: When you march up to attack a city, make its people an offer of peace.

verses.text: It shall be, if it makes you answer of peace, and opens to you, then it shall be, that all the people who are found therein shall become forced laborers to you, and shall serve you.
verses.explainer: If they accept and open their gates, all the people in it shall be subject to forced labor and shall work for you.

verses.text: If it will make no peace with you, but will make war against you, then you shall besiege it.
verses.explainer: If they refuse to make peace and they engage you in battle, lay siege to that city.

verses.text: When Yahweh your God delivers it into your hand, you shall strike every male of it with the edge of the sword;
verses.explainer: When the Lord your God delivers it into your hand, put to the sword all the men in it.

verses.text: but the women, the little ones, the livestock, and all that is in the city, even all its plunder, you shall take for plunder for yourself. You may use the plunder of your enemies, which Yahweh your God has given you.
verses.explainer: As for the women, the children, the livestock, and everything else in the city, you may take these as plunder for yourselves. And you may use the plunder the Lord your God gives you from your enemies.

verses.text: Thus you shall do to all the cities which are very far off from you, which are not of the cities of these nations.
verses.explainer: This is how you are to treat all the cities that are at a distance from you and do not belong to the nations nearby.

verses.text: But of the cities of these peoples, that Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes;
verses.explainer: However, in the cities of the nations the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance, do not leave alive anything that breathes.

verses.text: but you shall utterly destroy them: the Hittite, the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite; as Yahweh your God has commanded you;
verses.explainer: Completely destroy them -- the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites -- as the Lord your God has commanded you.

verses.text: that they not teach you to follow all their abominations, which they have done to their gods; so would you sin against Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Otherwise, they will teach you to follow all the detestable things they do in worshiping their gods, and you will sin against the Lord your God.

thenVsNow.then: The theological rationale for total destruction is explicitly stated: the Canaanites will teach Israel to adopt their practices. The concern is religious survival, not territorial expansion.
thenVsNow.now: These warfare texts remain among the most difficult in the Bible. They describe a specific, unrepeatable historical moment of divine judgment and should not be generalized into principles for contemporary warfare.

verses.text: When you shall besiege a city a long time, in making war against it to take it, you shall not destroy its trees by wielding an ax against them; for you may eat of them. You shall not cut them down; for is the tree of the field man, that it should be besieged by you?
verses.explainer: When you lay siege to a city for a long time, fighting against it to capture it, do not destroy its trees by putting an ax to them, because you can eat their fruit. Do not cut them down. Are the trees people, that you should besiege them?

verses.text: Only the trees that you know are not trees for food, you shall destroy and cut them down. You shall build bulwarks against the city that makes war with you, until it falls.
verses.explainer: However, you may cut down trees that you know are not fruit trees and use them to build siege works until the city at war with you falls.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This distinction between fruit trees and non-fruit trees during siege is unique in ancient warfare codes. Most ancient armies practiced 'scorched earth' tactics. Israel's law protects productive trees even from military necessity.

explainer.summary: A collection of laws addressing specific situations: unsolved murders (requiring a ritual with a heifer to remove communal bloodguilt), marrying captive women (providing protections for vulnerable women), the rights of firstborn sons (even from an unloved wife), and the punishment for rebellious sons. The chapter concludes with the law that a hanged body must not remain on the tree overnight -- a verse Paul applies to Christ.
themes: Communal responsibility for unsolved violence
Protection of captive women's dignity
Firstborn rights cannot be manipulated by favoritism
Parental authority and its limits
The curse of hanging on a tree
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 21 addresses situations where human relationships break down: murder without a known killer, marriage born from war, family favoritism, and incorrigible children. Each case tests whether Israel will apply justice consistently.

wordStudies.significance: The command that a hanged person's body must not remain overnight is connected to the declaration that such a person is 'under God's curse.' Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:13 to explain Christ's crucifixion: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us.'

verses.text: If someone is found slain in the land which Yahweh your God gives you to possess, lying in the field, and it isn’t known who has struck him;
verses.explainer: If someone is found slain, lying in a field in the land the Lord is giving you, and it is not known who the killer was --

verses.text: then your elders and your judges shall come out, and they shall measure to the cities which are around him who is slain.
verses.explainer: Your elders and judges shall go out and measure the distance from the body to the neighboring towns.

verses.text: It shall be that the elders of the city which is nearest to the slain man shall take a heifer of the herd, which hasn’t been worked with, and which has not drawn in the yoke.
verses.explainer: Then the elders of the town nearest the body shall take a heifer that has never been worked and has never worn a yoke.

verses.text: The elders of that city shall bring the heifer down to a valley with running water, which is neither plowed nor sown, and shall break the heifer’s neck there in the valley.
verses.explainer: They shall take the heifer to a valley that has not been plowed or planted and where there is a flowing stream. There they shall break the heifer's neck.

verses.text: The priests the sons of Levi shall come near; for them Yahweh your God has chosen to minister to him, and to bless in Yahweh’s name; and according to their word shall every controversy and every assault be decided.
verses.explainer: The Levitical priests shall step forward, for the Lord your God has chosen them to minister and to pronounce blessings in the name of the Lord and to decide all cases of dispute and assault.

verses.text: All the elders of that city, who are nearest to the slain man, shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the valley.
verses.explainer: Then all the elders of the town nearest the body shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken.

verses.text: They shall answer and say, “Our hands have not shed this blood, neither have our eyes seen it.
verses.explainer: And they shall declare: 'Our hands did not shed this blood, nor did our eyes see it done.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The handwashing ritual is a declaration of innocence -- the same gesture Pilate will use centuries later (Matthew 27:24). The public declaration transfers the guilt from the community to the sacrificial animal.

verses.text: Forgive, Yahweh, your people Israel, whom you have redeemed, and don’t allow innocent blood among your people Israel.” The blood shall be forgiven them.
verses.explainer: Accept this atonement for your people Israel, whom you have redeemed, Lord, and do not hold your people guilty of the blood of an innocent person. Then the bloodshed will be atoned for.

verses.text: So you shall put away the innocent blood from among you, when you shall do that which is right in Yahweh’s eyes.
verses.explainer: So you will purge from yourselves the guilt of shedding innocent blood, since you have done what is right in the eyes of the Lord.

verses.text: When you go out to battle against your enemies, and Yahweh your God delivers them into your hands, and you carry them away captive,
verses.explainer: When you go to war against your enemies and the Lord your God delivers them into your hands and you take captives --

verses.text: and see among the captives a beautiful woman, and you have a desire to her, and desire to take her as your wife;
verses.explainer: If you notice among the captives a beautiful woman and are attracted to her, you may take her as your wife.

verses.text: then you shall bring her home to your house. She shall shave her head and trim her nails.
verses.explainer: Bring her into your home and have her shave her head, trim her nails.

verses.text: She shall take off the clothing of her captivity, and shall remain in your house, and bewail her father and her mother a full month. After that you shall go in to her and be her husband, and she shall be your wife.
verses.explainer: And put aside the clothes she was wearing when captured. After she has lived in your house and mourned her father and mother for a full month, then you may go to her and be her husband and she shall be your wife.

thenVsNow.then: The month of mourning allowed the woman to grieve her losses before entering a new life. It also served as a cooling-off period for the man, ensuring the decision was not made in the heat of conquest.
thenVsNow.now: While the practice of taking captive wives is deeply problematic by modern standards, the Deuteronomic law was progressive for its time, providing protections that did not exist in surrounding cultures.

verses.text: It shall be, if you have no delight in her, then you shall let her go where she desires; but you shall not sell her at all for money. You shall not deal with her as a slave, because you have humbled her.
verses.explainer: If you are not pleased with her, let her go wherever she wishes. You must not sell her or treat her as a slave, since you have dishonored her.

verses.text: If a man has two wives, the one beloved, and the other hated, and they have borne him children, both the beloved and the hated; and if the firstborn son is hers who was hated;
verses.explainer: If a man has two wives, and he loves one but not the other, and both bear him sons but the firstborn is the son of the wife he does not love --

verses.text: then it shall be, in the day that he causes his sons to inherit that which he has, that he may not give the son of the beloved the rights of the firstborn before the son of the hated, who is the firstborn;
verses.explainer: When he wills his property to his sons, he must not give the rights of the firstborn to the son of the wife he loves in preference to his actual firstborn, the son of the wife he does not love.

verses.text: but he shall acknowledge the firstborn, the son of the hated, by giving him a double portion of all that he has; for he is the beginning of his strength. The right of the firstborn is his.
verses.explainer: He must acknowledge the son of his unloved wife as the firstborn by giving him a double share of all he has. That son is the first sign of his father's strength. The right of the firstborn belongs to him.

verses.text: If a man has a stubborn and rebellious son, who will not obey the voice of his father or the voice of his mother, and though they chasten him, will not listen to them;
verses.explainer: If someone has a stubborn and rebellious son who does not obey his father and mother and will not listen to them when they discipline him --

verses.text: then his father and his mother shall take hold of him and bring him out to the elders of his city, and to the gate of his place.
verses.explainer: His father and mother shall take hold of him and bring him to the elders at the gate of his town.

verses.text: They shall tell the elders of his city, “This our son is stubborn and rebellious. He will not obey our voice. He is a glutton and a drunkard.”
verses.explainer: They shall say to the elders: 'This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious. He will not obey us. He is a glutton and a drunkard.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Both parents must bring the accusation together, preventing one parent from acting alone. The case must go before the elders, removing the power of life and death from the father's sole authority. This is a constraint on patriarchal power, not an expansion of it.

verses.text: All the men of his city shall stone him to death with stones. So you shall remove the evil from among you. All Israel shall hear, and fear.
verses.explainer: Then all the men of his town shall stone him to death. You must purge the evil from among you. All Israel will hear of it and be afraid.

verses.text: If a man has committed a sin worthy of death, and he is put to death, and you hang him on a tree;
verses.explainer: If someone guilty of a capital offense is put to death and their body is exposed on a pole --

verses.text: his body shall not remain all night on the tree, but you shall surely bury him the same day; for he who is hanged is accursed of God; that you don’t defile your land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance.
verses.explainer: You must not leave the body hanging on the pole overnight. Be sure to bury it that same day, because anyone who is hung on a pole is under God's curse. You must not desecrate the land the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance.

crossReferences.reference: Galatians 3:13 -- Paul quotes this verse: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole.'
crossReferences.connection: Galatians 3:13 -- Paul quotes this verse: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole.'

explainer.summary: A diverse collection of laws covering returning lost property, helping fallen animals, prohibiting cross-dressing, protecting mother birds, building parapets on roofs, prohibiting various mixtures (seeds, plowing animals, clothing fibers), requiring tassels on garments, and extensive laws about sexual conduct including slander against a bride, adultery, rape, and seduction.
themes: Neighbor responsibility for property and safety
Order and distinction in creation
Sexual ethics and community protection
Protecting the vulnerable from false accusation
Consequences proportional to circumstances
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 22 demonstrates that covenant faithfulness extends to every area of daily life. From returning stray animals to building safe roofs to sexual conduct, nothing is outside the scope of covenant obligation.

wordStudies.significance: The prohibition against mixing seeds, animals, and fibers (vv.9-11) reflects the creation theology of Genesis 1, where God separates and categorizes. Maintaining distinctions honors the created order. The principle is not hygiene but theological -- respecting the boundaries God established.

verses.text: You shall not see your brother’s ox or his sheep go astray, and hide yourself from them. You shall surely bring them again to your brother.
verses.explainer: If you see your fellow Israelite's ox or sheep straying, do not ignore it but be sure to take it back to its owner.

thenVsNow.then: Returning stray animals was costly in time and effort. This law makes active helpfulness a legal obligation, not merely a moral suggestion.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of active responsibility for a neighbor's property challenges the 'not my problem' mentality. Community membership carries obligations that extend beyond personal convenience.

verses.text: If your brother isn’t near to you, or if you don’t know him, then you shall bring it home to your house, and it shall be with you until your brother comes looking for it, and you shall restore it to him.
verses.explainer: If they do not live near you or you do not know who owns it, take it home with you and keep it until they come looking for it. Then give it back.

verses.text: So you shall do with his donkey. So you shall do with his garment. So you shall do with every lost thing of your brother’s, which he has lost, and you have found. You may not hide yourself.
verses.explainer: Do the same if you find their donkey or cloak or anything else they have lost. Do not ignore it.

verses.text: You shall not see your brother’s donkey or his ox fallen down by the way, and hide yourself from them. You shall surely help him to lift them up again.
verses.explainer: If you see your fellow Israelite's donkey or ox fallen on the road, do not ignore it. Help the owner get it to its feet.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 23:5 -- Even if the animal belongs to your enemy, you must help. The obligation extends beyond friendly relationships.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 23:5 -- Even if the animal belongs to your enemy, you must help. The obligation extends beyond friendly relationships.

verses.text: A woman shall not wear men’s clothing, neither shall a man put on women’s clothing; for whoever does these things is an abomination to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: A woman must not wear men's clothing, nor a man wear women's clothing, for the Lord your God detests anyone who does this.

verses.text: If you come across a bird’s nest on the way, in any tree or on the ground, with young ones or eggs, and the hen sitting on the young, or on the eggs, you shall not take the hen with the young.
verses.explainer: If you come across a bird's nest beside the road, either in a tree or on the ground, and the mother is sitting on the young or on the eggs, do not take the mother with the young.

verses.text: You shall surely let the hen go, but the young you may take for yourself; that it may be well with you, and that you may prolong your days.
verses.explainer: You may take the young, but be sure to let the mother go, so that it may go well with you and you may have a long life.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This law balances human need (taking the eggs or young) with ecological sustainability (releasing the mother to breed again). It is one of the earliest environmental protection laws. Jewish tradition considered it the 'lightest' of all commandments, yet it still carries the reward of long life.

verses.text: When you build a new house, then you shall make a railing around your roof, so that you don’t bring blood on your house if anyone falls from there.
verses.explainer: When you build a new house, make a parapet around your roof so that you may not bring the guilt of bloodshed on your house if someone falls from the roof.

verses.text: You shall not sow your vineyard with two kinds of seed, lest all the fruit be defiled, the seed which you have sown, and the increase of the vineyard.
verses.explainer: Do not plant two kinds of seed in your vineyard; if you do, not only the crops you plant but also the fruit of the vineyard will be defiled.

verses.text: You shall not plow with an ox and a donkey together.
verses.explainer: Do not plow with an ox and a donkey yoked together.

crossReferences.reference: 2 Corinthians 6:14 -- Paul applies this image metaphorically: 'Do not be yoked together with unbelievers.' The unequal yoke represents a mismatch that harms both parties.
crossReferences.connection: 2 Corinthians 6:14 -- Paul applies this image metaphorically: 'Do not be yoked together with unbelievers.' The unequal yoke represents a mismatch that harms both parties.

verses.text: You shall not wear clothes of wool and linen woven together.
verses.explainer: Do not wear clothes of wool and linen woven together.

verses.text: You shall make yourselves fringes on the four corners of your cloak with which you cover yourself.
verses.explainer: Make tassels on the four corners of the cloak you wear.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 9:20 -- The woman with the hemorrhage touched 'the edge of his cloak' -- likely the tassels (tzitzit) prescribed here, which represented covenant commitment.
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 9:20 -- The woman with the hemorrhage touched 'the edge of his cloak' -- likely the tassels (tzitzit) prescribed here, which represented covenant commitment.

verses.text: If any man takes a wife, and goes in to her, hates her,
verses.explainer: If a man takes a wife and, after sleeping with her, dislikes her --

verses.text: accuses her of shameful things, and gives her a bad name, and says, “I took this woman, and when I came near to her, I didn’t find in her the tokens of virginity”;
verses.explainer: And slanders her and gives her a bad name, saying, 'I married this woman, but when I approached her, I did not find proof of her virginity' --

verses.text: then the young lady’s father and mother shall take and bring the tokens of the young lady’s virginity to the elders of the city in the gate.
verses.explainer: Then the young woman's father and mother shall bring to the town elders at the gate proof that she was a virgin.

verses.text: The young lady’s father shall tell the elders, “I gave my daughter to this man as his wife, and he hates her.
verses.explainer: Her father will say to the elders, 'I gave my daughter in marriage to this man, but he dislikes her.'

verses.text: Behold, he has accused her of shameful things, saying, ‘I didn’t find in your daughter the tokens of virginity;’ and yet these are the tokens of my daughter’s virginity.” They shall spread the cloth before the elders of the city.
verses.explainer: Now he has slandered her and said, 'I did not find your daughter to be a virgin.' But here is the proof of my daughter's virginity. Then her parents shall display the cloth before the elders of the town.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'proof of virginity' was the bloodstained wedding-night sheet preserved by the bride's family as legal protection. This system, while alien to modern sensibilities, provided women with a concrete defense against false accusation.

verses.text: The elders of that city shall take the man and chastise him.
verses.explainer: And the elders shall take the man and punish him.

verses.text: They shall fine him one hundred shekels of silver, and give them to the father of the young lady, because he has given a bad name to a virgin of Israel. She shall be his wife. He may not put her away all his days.
verses.explainer: They shall fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the young woman's father, because this man has given an Israelite virgin a bad name. She shall continue to be his wife; he must not divorce her as long as he lives.

verses.text: But if this thing is true, that the tokens of virginity were not found in the young lady;
verses.explainer: If, however, the charge is true and no proof of the young woman's virginity can be found --

verses.text: then they shall bring out the young lady to the door of her father’s house, and the men of her city shall stone her to death with stones, because she has done folly in Israel, to play the prostitute in her father’s house. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: She shall be brought to the door of her father's house and there the men of her town shall stone her to death. She has done an outrageous thing in Israel by being promiscuous while still in her father's house. You must purge the evil from among you.

verses.text: If a man is found lying with a woman married to a husband, then they shall both die, the man who lay with the woman and the woman. So you shall remove the evil from Israel.
verses.explainer: If a man is found sleeping with another man's wife, both the man who slept with her and the woman must die. You must purge the evil from Israel.

verses.text: If there is a young lady who is a virgin pledged to be married to a husband, and a man finds her in the city, and lies with her;
verses.explainer: If a man happens to meet in a town a virgin pledged to be married and he sleeps with her --

verses.text: then you shall bring them both out to the gate of that city, and you shall stone them to death with stones; the lady, because she didn’t cry, being in the city; and the man, because he has humbled his neighbor’s wife. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: You shall take both of them to the gate of that town and stone them to death -- the young woman because she was in a town and did not scream for help, and the man because he violated another man's wife.

verses.text: But if the man finds the lady who is pledged to be married in the field, and the man forces her, and lies with her; then only the man who lay with her shall die;
verses.explainer: But if out in the country a man happens to meet a young woman pledged to be married and rapes her, only the man who has done this shall die.

verses.text: but to the lady you shall do nothing. There is in the lady no sin worthy of death; for as when a man rises against his neighbor, and kills him, even so is this matter;
verses.explainer: Do nothing to the woman; she has committed no sin deserving death. This case is like that of someone who attacks and murders a neighbor.

verses.text: for he found her in the field, the pledged to be married lady cried, and there was no one to save her.
verses.explainer: For the man found her out in the country, and though the betrothed woman screamed, there was no one to rescue her.

verses.text: If a man finds a lady who is a virgin, who is not pledged to be married, grabs her, and lies with her, and they are found;
verses.explainer: If a man happens to meet a virgin who is not pledged to be married and rapes her and they are discovered --

verses.text: then the man who lay with her shall give to the lady’s father fifty shekels of silver. She shall be his wife, because he has humbled her. He may not put her away all his days.
verses.explainer: He shall pay her father fifty shekels of silver. He must marry the young woman, for he has violated her. He can never divorce her as long as he lives.

thenVsNow.then: In a society where a non-virgin woman had severely diminished marriage prospects, this law forced the violator to provide lifelong financial support. The inability to divorce ensured the woman's economic security.
thenVsNow.now: This provision strikes modern readers as unjust to the woman. It must be understood within a social system where an unmarried non-virgin faced destitution. The law prioritizes the woman's economic survival within the constraints of its cultural context.

verses.text: A man shall not take his father’s wife, and shall not uncover his father’s skirt.
verses.explainer: A man is not to marry his father's wife; he must not dishonor his father's bed.

explainer.summary: This chapter covers who may and may not enter the assembly of the Lord, camp hygiene, protection for runaway slaves, prohibition of temple prostitution, regulations on interest-free loans to fellow Israelites, and the obligation to fulfill vows promptly. The mix of ritual, hygienic, social, and economic laws reflects Deuteronomy's conviction that holiness encompasses every dimension of life.
themes: Who belongs in God's assembly
Camp holiness and hygiene
Protection for escaped slaves
No interest on loans to fellow Israelites
Vows must be fulfilled
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 23 continues the miscellaneous laws section, moving between ritual status, military hygiene, social ethics, and economic justice. The variety demonstrates that no category of life is exempt from covenant regulation.

wordStudies.significance: The 'assembly of the Lord' refers to the formal worship community, not simply physical presence in the camp. Exclusion from the assembly meant inability to participate in worship and possibly hold civic office, not expulsion from Israel entirely.

verses.text: He who is emasculated by crushing or cutting shall not enter into Yahweh’s assembly.
verses.explainer: No one who has been emasculated by crushing or cutting may enter the assembly of the Lord.

verses.text: A person born of a forbidden union shall not enter into Yahweh’s assembly; even to the tenth generation shall no one of his enter into Yahweh’s assembly.
verses.explainer: No one born of a forbidden marriage nor any of their descendants may enter the assembly of the Lord, not even in the tenth generation.

verses.text: An Ammonite or a Moabite shall not enter into Yahweh’s assembly; even to the tenth generation shall no one belonging to them enter into Yahweh’s assembly forever;
verses.explainer: No Ammonite or Moabite or any of their descendants may enter the assembly of the Lord, not even in the tenth generation.

crossReferences.reference: Ruth 1:22 -- Ruth the Moabite enters Israel and becomes David's great-grandmother. Her inclusion challenges a rigid reading of this exclusion and demonstrates God's grace transcending legal categories.
crossReferences.connection: Ruth 1:22 -- Ruth the Moabite enters Israel and becomes David's great-grandmother. Her inclusion challenges a rigid reading of this exclusion and demonstrates God's grace transcending legal categories.

verses.text: because they didn’t meet you with bread and with water on the way, when you came out of Egypt, and because they hired against you Balaam the son of Beor from Pethor of Mesopotamia, to curse you.
verses.explainer: For they did not come to meet you with bread and water on your way when you came out of Egypt, and they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Aram Naharaim to pronounce a curse on you.

verses.text: Nevertheless Yahweh your God wouldn’t listen to Balaam; but Yahweh your God turned the curse into a blessing to you, because Yahweh your God loved you.
verses.explainer: However, the Lord your God would not listen to Balaam but turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the Lord your God loves you.

verses.text: You shall not seek their peace nor their prosperity all your days forever.
verses.explainer: Do not seek a treaty of friendship with them as long as you live.

verses.text: You shall not abhor an Edomite; for he is your brother. You shall not abhor an Egyptian, because you lived as a foreigner in his land.
verses.explainer: Do not despise an Edomite, for the Edomites are your relatives. Do not despise an Egyptian, because you resided as foreigners in their country.

thenVsNow.then: Despite Egypt's oppression, Israel must not despise Egyptians because Egypt provided refuge during the famine. Gratitude for past hospitality overrides resentment for past oppression.
thenVsNow.now: The command not to despise even former oppressors is remarkable. It prioritizes gratitude over grievance and refuses to let history's worst moments define the entire relationship.

verses.text: The children of the third generation who are born to them may enter into Yahweh’s assembly.
verses.explainer: The third generation of children born to them may enter the assembly of the Lord.

verses.text: When you go out and camp against your enemies, then you shall keep yourselves from every evil thing.
verses.explainer: When you are encamped against your enemies, keep away from everything impure.

verses.text: If there is among you any man who is not clean by reason of that which happens him by night, then shall he go outside of the camp. He shall not come within the camp;
verses.explainer: If one of your men is unclean because of a nocturnal emission, he is to go outside the camp and stay there.

verses.text: but it shall be, when evening comes, he shall bathe himself in water. When the sun is down, he shall come within the camp.
verses.explainer: But as evening approaches he is to wash himself, and at sunset he may return to the camp.

verses.text: You shall have a place also outside of the camp where you go relieve yourself.
verses.explainer: Designate a place outside the camp where you can go to relieve yourself.

verses.text: You shall have a trowel among your weapons. It shall be, when you relieve yourself, you shall dig with it, and shall turn back and cover your excrement;
verses.explainer: As part of your equipment have something to dig with, and when you relieve yourself, dig a hole and cover up your excrement.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This is a public health regulation embedded in theological reasoning. The military camp must be sanitary because God walks among the troops. The theological motivation produces practical hygiene -- centuries before germ theory.

verses.text: for Yahweh your God walks in the middle of your camp, to deliver you, and to give up your enemies before you. Therefore your camp shall be holy, that he may not see an unclean thing in you, and turn away from you.
verses.explainer: For the Lord your God moves about in your camp to protect you and to deliver your enemies to you. Your camp must be holy, so that he will not see among you anything indecent and turn away from you.

verses.text: You shall not deliver to his master a servant who has escaped from his master to you.
verses.explainer: If a slave has taken refuge with you, do not hand them over to their master.

verses.text: He shall dwell with you, among you, in the place which he shall choose within one of your gates, where it pleases him best. You shall not oppress him.
verses.explainer: Let them live among you wherever they like and in whatever town they choose. Do not oppress them.

verses.text: There shall be no prostitute of the daughters of Israel, neither shall there be a sodomite of the sons of Israel.
verses.explainer: No Israelite man or woman is to become a shrine prostitute.

verses.text: You shall not bring the hire of a prostitute, or the wages of a male prostitute, into the house of Yahweh your God for any vow; for both of these are an abomination to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: You must not bring the earnings of a female prostitute or of a male prostitute into the house of the Lord your God to pay any vow, because the Lord your God detests them both.

verses.text: You shall not lend on interest to your brother; interest of money, interest of food, interest of anything that is lent on interest.
verses.explainer: Do not charge a fellow Israelite interest, whether on money or food or anything else that may earn interest.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 22:25 -- The prohibition against interest on loans to fellow Israelites is rooted in the principle that economic relationships within the covenant community should be governed by generosity, not profit.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 22:25 -- The prohibition against interest on loans to fellow Israelites is rooted in the principle that economic relationships within the covenant community should be governed by generosity, not profit.

verses.text: You may lend on interest to a foreigner; but to your brother you shall not lend on interest, that Yahweh your God may bless you in all that you put your hand to, in the land where you go in to possess it.
verses.explainer: You may charge a foreigner interest, but not a fellow Israelite, so that the Lord your God may bless you in everything you put your hand to in the land you are entering to possess.

verses.text: When you vow a vow to Yahweh your God, you shall not be slack to pay it; for Yahweh your God will surely require it of you; and it would be sin in you.
verses.explainer: If you make a vow to the Lord your God, do not be slow to pay it, for the Lord your God will certainly demand it of you and you will be guilty of sin.

verses.text: But if you refrain from making a vow, it shall be no sin in you.
verses.explainer: But if you refrain from making a vow, you will not be guilty.

verses.text: You shall observe and do that which has gone out of your lips. Whatever you have vowed to Yahweh your God as a freewill offering, which you have promised with your mouth, you must do.
verses.explainer: Whatever your lips utter you must be sure to do, because you made your vow freely to the Lord your God with your own mouth.

verses.text: When you come into your neighbor’s vineyard, then you may eat of grapes your fill at your own pleasure; but you shall not put any in your container.
verses.explainer: If you enter your neighbor's vineyard, you may eat all the grapes you want, but do not put any in your basket.

thenVsNow.then: The right to eat from a neighbor's field while passing through provided for travelers and the hungry. The prohibition against filling a basket prevented abuse -- taking more than an immediate need.
thenVsNow.now: This balances generosity with property rights: hospitality for the hungry traveler, but not a license for exploitation.

verses.text: When you come into your neighbor’s standing grain, then you may pluck the ears with your hand; but you shall not move a sickle to your neighbor’s standing grain.
verses.explainer: If you enter your neighbor's grainfield, you may pick kernels with your hands, but you must not put a sickle to their standing grain.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 12:1 -- Jesus' disciples pick grain on the Sabbath, an act permitted by this law but challenged by Pharisaic interpretation of Sabbath rules.
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 12:1 -- Jesus' disciples pick grain on the Sabbath, an act permitted by this law but challenged by Pharisaic interpretation of Sabbath rules.

explainer.summary: Moses addresses divorce regulations (a man who divorces his wife cannot remarry her if she has married another), exemption from military service for newlyweds, prohibition of kidnapping, treatment of skin diseases, justice in lending (do not take a millstone as collateral), wages for laborers (pay them daily), individual responsibility for sin, and care for foreigners, orphans, and widows. The chapter repeatedly grounds social ethics in the memory of Egyptian slavery.
themes: Divorce regulation -- not endorsement
Protect the newlywed, the worker, and the vulnerable
No kidnapping -- it is a capital offense
Leave gleanings for the poor
Remember you were slaves in Egypt
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 24 is the most concentrated collection of social justice legislation in Deuteronomy. Nearly every law protects someone vulnerable: the divorced woman, the new husband, the laborer, the debtor, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow.

wordStudies.significance: The grounds for divorce -- 'erwat davar' (something indecent) -- were debated intensely. The school of Shammai restricted it to sexual immorality. The school of Hillel extended it to any displeasing behavior. Jesus was asked about this debate in Matthew 19:3-9.

verses.text: When a man takes a wife and marries her, then it shall be, if she finds no favor in his eyes, because he has found some unseemly thing in her, that he shall write her a bill of divorce, and put it in her hand, and send her out of his house.
verses.explainer: If a man marries a woman who becomes displeasing to him because he finds something indecent about her, and he writes her a certificate of divorce, gives it to her and sends her from his house --

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 19:7-8 -- Jesus explains: 'Moses permitted you to divorce your wives because your hearts were hard. But it was not this way from the beginning.'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 19:7-8 -- Jesus explains: 'Moses permitted you to divorce your wives because your hearts were hard. But it was not this way from the beginning.'

verses.text: When she has departed out of his house, she may go and be another man’s wife.
verses.explainer: And if after she leaves his house she becomes the wife of another man --

verses.text: If the latter husband hates her, and write her a bill of divorce, and puts it in her hand, and sends her out of his house; or if the latter husband die, who took her to be his wife;
verses.explainer: And her second husband dislikes her and writes her a certificate of divorce, or if he dies --

verses.text: her former husband, who sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before Yahweh. You shall not cause the land to sin, which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance.
verses.explainer: Then her first husband, who divorced her, is not allowed to marry her again after she has been defiled. That would be detestable in the eyes of the Lord. Do not bring sin upon the land the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance.

thenVsNow.then: The prohibition against remarriage to the first husband prevents women from being traded back and forth as property. It also prevents a man from divorcing his wife, letting another man marry her, and then reclaiming her.
thenVsNow.now: This regulation demonstrates that even in the ancient world, concern existed about the commodification of women in marriage. The law limits male prerogative, not female freedom.

verses.text: When a man takes a new wife, he shall not go out in the army, neither shall he be assigned any business. He shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer his wife whom he has taken.
verses.explainer: If a man has recently married, he must not be sent to war or have any other duty laid on him. For one year he is to be free to stay at home and bring happiness to the wife he has married.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: A full year's exemption from military and civic duties for newlyweds is extraordinarily generous. It prioritizes marriage over military need, reflecting the conviction that stable families are the foundation of a stable society.

verses.text: No man shall take the mill or the upper millstone as a pledge; for he takes a life in pledge.
verses.explainer: Do not take a pair of millstones -- not even the upper one -- as security for a debt, because that would be taking a person's livelihood as security.

verses.text: If a man is found stealing any of his brothers of the children of Israel, and he deals with him as a slave, or sells him; then that thief shall die. So you shall remove the evil from among you.
verses.explainer: If someone is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite and treating or selling them as a slave, the kidnapper must die. You must purge the evil from among you.

verses.text: Be careful in the plague of leprosy, that you observe diligently, and do according to all that the priests the Levites teach you. As I commanded them, so you shall observe to do.
verses.explainer: In cases of defiling skin diseases, be very careful to do exactly as the Levitical priests instruct you. You must follow carefully what I have commanded them.

verses.text: Remember what Yahweh your God did to Miriam, by the way as you came out of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Remember what the Lord your God did to Miriam along the way after you came out of Egypt.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 12:10 -- Miriam was struck with a skin disease for challenging Moses' authority. The reference serves as a warning that even leaders are subject to divine discipline.
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 12:10 -- Miriam was struck with a skin disease for challenging Moses' authority. The reference serves as a warning that even leaders are subject to divine discipline.

verses.text: When you lend your neighbor any kind of loan, you shall not go into his house to get his pledge.
verses.explainer: When you make a loan of any kind to your neighbor, do not go into their house to get what is offered to you as a pledge.

verses.text: You shall stand outside, and the man to whom you lend shall bring the pledge outside to you.
verses.explainer: Stay outside and let the neighbor bring the pledge out to you.

verses.text: If he is a poor man, you shall not sleep with his pledge.
verses.explainer: If the neighbor is poor, do not go to sleep with their pledge in your possession.

verses.text: You shall surely restore to him the pledge when the sun goes down, that he may sleep in his garment, and bless you. It shall be righteousness to you before Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Return their cloak by sunset so that your neighbor may sleep in it. Then they will thank you, and it will be regarded as a righteous act in the sight of the Lord your God.

crossReferences.reference: Amos 2:8 -- Amos condemns Israel for lying on garments taken as pledges beside altars -- directly violating this law.
crossReferences.connection: Amos 2:8 -- Amos condemns Israel for lying on garments taken as pledges beside altars -- directly violating this law.

verses.text: You shall not oppress a hired servant who is poor and needy, whether he is one of your brothers, or one of the foreigners who are in your land within your gates.
verses.explainer: Do not take advantage of a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether that worker is a fellow Israelite or a foreigner residing in one of your towns.

verses.text: In his day you shall give him his hire, neither shall the sun go down on it; for he is poor, and sets his heart on it; lest he cry against you to Yahweh, and it be sin to you.
verses.explainer: Pay them their wages each day before sunset, because they are poor and are counting on it. Otherwise they may cry to the Lord against you, and you will be guilty of sin.

thenVsNow.then: Day laborers lived hand-to-mouth. Withholding a day's wages meant a family went hungry that night. The law treats delayed payment as a form of theft.
thenVsNow.now: Wage theft remains one of the most common forms of exploitation globally. The principle of timely, fair payment is not archaic but urgently relevant.

verses.text: The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers. Every man shall be put to death for his own sin.
verses.explainer: Parents are not to be put to death for their children, nor children put to death for their parents; each will die for their own sin.

verses.text: You shall not deprive the foreigner or the fatherless of justice, nor take a widow’s clothing in pledge;
verses.explainer: Do not deprive the foreigner or the fatherless of justice, or take the cloak of the widow as a pledge.

verses.text: but you shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and Yahweh your God redeemed you there. Therefore I command you to do this thing.
verses.explainer: Remember that you were slaves in Egypt and the Lord your God redeemed you from there. That is why I command you to do this.

verses.text: When you reap your harvest in your field, and have forgotten a sheaf in the field, you shall not go again to get it. It shall be for the foreigner, for the fatherless, and for the widow; that Yahweh your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.
verses.explainer: When you are harvesting in your field and you overlook a sheaf, do not go back to get it. Leave it for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, so that the Lord your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.

crossReferences.reference: Ruth 2:2-3 -- Ruth gleans in Boaz's field, exercising the right established in this law. The gleaning laws provide the narrative mechanism for Ruth's survival and eventual marriage to Boaz.
crossReferences.connection: Ruth 2:2-3 -- Ruth gleans in Boaz's field, exercising the right established in this law. The gleaning laws provide the narrative mechanism for Ruth's survival and eventual marriage to Boaz.

verses.text: When you beat your olive tree, you shall not go over the boughs again. It shall be for the foreigner, for the fatherless, and for the widow.
verses.explainer: When you beat the olives from your trees, do not go over the branches a second time. Leave what remains for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.

verses.text: When you harvest your vineyard, you shall not glean it after yourselves. It shall be for the foreigner, for the fatherless, and for the widow.
verses.explainer: When you harvest the grapes in your vineyard, do not go over the vines again. Leave what remains for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.

verses.text: You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt. Therefore I command you to do this thing.
verses.explainer: Remember that you were slaves in Egypt. That is why I command you to do this.

explainer.summary: Laws about limiting punishment (no more than forty lashes), not muzzling an ox while it treads grain, levirate marriage (a brother's duty to marry his deceased brother's widow), penalties for a woman who seizes a man's genitals during a fight, honest weights and measures, and the command to blot out the memory of Amalek.
themes: Limits on punishment -- preserving dignity
The working animal deserves to eat
Levirate marriage preserves a name
Honest business practices
Remember and destroy Amalek
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 25 continues the miscellaneous laws, moving from judicial punishment to animal welfare to family law to commercial ethics to national memory. The common thread is justice -- in punishment, in labor, in family, in commerce, and in war.

wordStudies.significance: Levirate marriage (from Latin 'levir,' brother-in-law) required a man to marry his deceased brother's childless widow and produce an heir in the dead brother's name. The practice preserved the family name, kept land within the family, and provided for the widow.

verses.text: If there is a controversy between men, and they come to judgment, and the judges judge them; then they shall justify the righteous, and condemn the wicked.
verses.explainer: When people have a dispute, they are to take it to court and the judges will decide the case, acquitting the innocent and condemning the guilty.

verses.text: It shall be, if the wicked man is worthy to be beaten, that the judge shall cause him to lie down, and to be beaten before his face, according to his wickedness, by number.
verses.explainer: If the guilty person deserves to be beaten, the judge shall make them lie down and have them flogged in his presence with the number of lashes the crime deserves.

verses.text: He may sentence him to no more than forty stripes. He shall not give more; lest, if he should give more, and beat him more than that many stripes, then your brother will be degraded in your sight.
verses.explainer: But the judge must not impose more than forty lashes. If the guilty party is flogged more than that, your fellow Israelite will be degraded in your eyes.

crossReferences.reference: 2 Corinthians 11:24 -- Paul received 'forty lashes minus one' five times. Jewish practice reduced the maximum to thirty-nine to ensure the limit was never accidentally exceeded.
crossReferences.connection: 2 Corinthians 11:24 -- Paul received 'forty lashes minus one' five times. Jewish practice reduced the maximum to thirty-nine to ensure the limit was never accidentally exceeded.

verses.text: You shall not muzzle the ox when he treads out the grain.
verses.explainer: Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Corinthians 9:9-10 -- Paul applies this law to church ministry: 'Is it about oxen that God is concerned? Surely he says this for us, because those who plow and thresh should share in the harvest.'
crossReferences.connection: 1 Corinthians 9:9-10 -- Paul applies this law to church ministry: 'Is it about oxen that God is concerned? Surely he says this for us, because those who plow and thresh should share in the harvest.'

verses.text: If brothers dwell together, and one of them dies, and has no son, the wife of the dead shall not be married outside to a stranger. Her husband’s brother shall go in to her, and take her as his wife, and perform the duty of a husband’s brother to her.
verses.explainer: If brothers are living together and one of them dies without a son, his widow must not marry outside the family. Her husband's brother shall take her and marry her and fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to her.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Levirate marriage provided social security for the widow and preserved the dead man's name and property within the family. Without it, the widow faced destitution and the dead man's line was extinguished.

verses.text: It shall be that the firstborn whom she bears shall succeed in the name of his brother who is dead, that his name not be blotted out of Israel.
verses.explainer: The first son she bears shall carry on the name of the dead brother so that his name will not be blotted out from Israel.

verses.text: If the man doesn’t want to take his brother’s wife, then his brother’s wife shall go up to the gate to the elders, and say, “My husband’s brother refuses to raise up to his brother a name in Israel. He will not perform the duty of a husband’s brother to me.”
verses.explainer: However, if a man does not want to marry his brother's wife, she shall go to the elders at the town gate and say, 'My husband's brother refuses to carry on his brother's name in Israel. He will not fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to me.'

verses.text: Then the elders of his city shall call him, and speak to him: and if he stands and says, “I don’t want to take her”;
verses.explainer: Then the elders of his town shall summon him and talk to him. If he persists in saying, 'I do not want to marry her' --

verses.text: then his brother’s wife shall come to him in the presence of the elders, and loose his shoe from off his foot, and spit in his face. She shall answer and say, “So shall it be done to the man who does not build up his brother’s house.”
verses.explainer: His brother's wife shall go up to him in the presence of the elders, take off one of his sandals, spit in his face and say, 'This is what is done to the man who will not build up his brother's family line.'

thenVsNow.then: The public shaming ceremony was a social penalty for refusing family obligation. The removal of the sandal symbolized the surrender of property rights (Ruth 4:7-8). The spit expressed communal contempt.
thenVsNow.now: While the specific ritual is culturally bound, the principle that refusing to care for vulnerable family members deserves social censure transcends the ancient context.

verses.text: His name shall be called in Israel, “The house of him who had his shoe removed.”
verses.explainer: That man's line shall be known in Israel as 'The Family of the Unsandaled.'

verses.text: When men strive against each other, and the wife of one draws near to deliver her husband out of the hand of him who strikes him, and puts out her hand, and takes him by his private parts,
verses.explainer: If two men are fighting and the wife of one of them comes to rescue her husband from his assailant, and she reaches out and seizes him by his private parts --

verses.text: then you shall cut off her hand. Your eye shall have no pity.
verses.explainer: You shall cut off her hand. Show her no pity.

verses.text: You shall not have in your bag diverse weights, one heavy and one light.
verses.explainer: Do not have two differing weights in your bag -- one heavy, one light.

verses.text: You shall not have in your house diverse measures, one large and one small.
verses.explainer: Do not have two differing measures in your house -- one large, one small.

verses.text: You shall have a perfect and just weight. You shall have a perfect and just measure, that your days may be long in the land which Yahweh your God gives you.
verses.explainer: You must have accurate and honest weights and measures, so that you may live long in the land the Lord your God is giving you.

verses.text: For all who do such things, all who do unrighteously, are an abomination to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: For the Lord your God detests anyone who does these things, anyone who deals dishonestly.

crossReferences.reference: Proverbs 11:1 -- 'Dishonest scales are an abomination to the Lord, but accurate weights find his favor.' The prophets and wisdom literature repeatedly echo this concern.
crossReferences.connection: Proverbs 11:1 -- 'Dishonest scales are an abomination to the Lord, but accurate weights find his favor.' The prophets and wisdom literature repeatedly echo this concern.

verses.text: Remember what Amalek did to you by the way as you came out of Egypt;
verses.explainer: Remember what the Amalekites did to you along the way when you came out of Egypt.

verses.text: how he met you by the way, and struck the rearmost of you, all who were feeble behind you, when you were faint and weary; and he didn’t fear God.
verses.explainer: When you were weary and worn out, they met you on your journey and attacked all who were lagging behind; they had no fear of God.

verses.text: Therefore it shall be, when Yahweh your God has given you rest from all your enemies all around, in the land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance to possess it, that you shall blot out the memory of Amalek from under the sky. You shall not forget.
verses.explainer: When the Lord your God gives you rest from all the enemies around you in the land he is giving you to possess as an inheritance, you shall blot out the name of Amalek from under heaven. Do not forget!

crossReferences.reference: 1 Samuel 15:2-3 -- God commands Saul to carry out this sentence against Amalek. Saul's failure to do so completely costs him the kingdom.
crossReferences.connection: 1 Samuel 15:2-3 -- God commands Saul to carry out this sentence against Amalek. Saul's failure to do so completely costs him the kingdom.

explainer.summary: Moses concludes the law section with two liturgical declarations. First, when presenting firstfruits, the worshiper recites a creed beginning 'My father was a wandering Aramean' -- a compressed version of Israel's history from Jacob through the Exodus to the present. Second, every third year, the worshiper declares that tithes have been properly distributed to the Levite, foreigner, orphan, and widow. The chapter ends with a mutual declaration: Israel declares God as their God, and God declares Israel as his people.
themes: The firstfruits creed -- historical identity through worship
Proper tithe distribution declared before God
Mutual covenant declaration
Worship as storytelling
The end of the law section
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 26 concludes the specific laws (chapters 12-26) with liturgical acts that summarize everything. The firstfruits creed retells the story; the tithe declaration confirms obedience. Together they bind identity, gratitude, and ethics into a single act of worship.

wordStudies.translatedAs: My father was a wandering Aramean
wordStudies.significance: This phrase opens Israel's oldest creed. The 'wandering Aramean' is Jacob, who went to Aram (Mesopotamia) and later descended to Egypt. The creed defines Israel's identity not through power but through vulnerability: we were nomads, slaves, and refugees whom God rescued.

verses.text: It shall be, when you have come in to the land which Yahweh your God gives you for an inheritance, possess it, and dwell in it,
verses.explainer: When you have entered the land the Lord your God is giving you as an inheritance and have taken possession of it and settled in it --

verses.text: that you shall take some of the first of all the fruit of the ground, which you shall bring in from your land that Yahweh your God gives you. You shall put it in a basket, and shall go to the place which Yahweh your God shall choose, to cause his name to dwell there.
verses.explainer: Take some of the firstfruits of all that you produce from the soil of the land the Lord is giving you and put them in a basket. Then go to the place the Lord will choose as a dwelling for his Name.

verses.text: You shall come to the priest who shall be in those days, and tell him, “I profess today to Yahweh your God, that I have come to the land which Yahweh swore to our fathers to give us.”
verses.explainer: Go to the priest in office at the time and say: 'I declare today to the Lord your God that I have come to the land the Lord swore to our ancestors to give us.'

verses.text: The priest shall take the basket out of your hand, and set it down before Yahweh your God’s altar.
verses.explainer: The priest shall take the basket from your hands and set it down in front of the altar of the Lord your God.

verses.text: You shall answer and say before Yahweh your God, “My father was a Syrian ready to perish. He went down into Egypt, and lived there, few in number. There he became a great, mighty, and populous nation.
verses.explainer: Then you shall declare before the Lord your God: 'My father was a wandering Aramean, and he went down into Egypt with a few people and lived there and became a great nation, powerful and numerous.'

verses.text: The Egyptians mistreated us, afflicted us, and imposed hard labor on us.
verses.explainer: But the Egyptians mistreated us and made us suffer, subjecting us to harsh labor.

verses.text: Then we cried to Yahweh, the God of our fathers. Yahweh heard our voice, and saw our affliction, our toil, and our oppression.
verses.explainer: Then we cried out to the Lord, the God of our ancestors, and the Lord heard our voice and saw our misery, toil, and oppression.

verses.text: Yahweh brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand, with an outstretched arm, with great terror, with signs, and with wonders;
verses.explainer: So the Lord brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, with great terror and with signs and wonders.

crossReferences.reference: Nehemiah 9:9-11 -- Nehemiah's great prayer follows the same narrative structure as this creed, showing its influence on later Israelite worship.
crossReferences.connection: Nehemiah 9:9-11 -- Nehemiah's great prayer follows the same narrative structure as this creed, showing its influence on later Israelite worship.

verses.text: and he has brought us into this place, and has given us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey.
verses.explainer: He brought us to this place and gave us this land, a land flowing with milk and honey.

verses.text: Now, behold, I have brought the first of the fruit of the ground, which you, Yahweh, have given me.” You shall set it down before Yahweh your God, and worship before Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: And now I bring the firstfruits of the soil that you, Lord, have given me.' Place the basket before the Lord your God and bow down before him.

thenVsNow.then: The firstfruits offering returned the beginning of the harvest to God, acknowledging that the entire harvest was a gift. The creed prevented the worshiper from thinking the produce was self-generated.
thenVsNow.now: Telling the story of God's provision as part of giving transforms generosity from obligation to gratitude. When we remember where we came from, giving becomes natural.

verses.text: You shall rejoice in all the good which Yahweh your God has given to you, and to your house, you, and the Levite, and the foreigner who is among you.
verses.explainer: Then you and the Levites and the foreigners residing among you shall rejoice in all the good things the Lord your God has given to you and your household.

verses.text: When you have finished tithing all the tithe of your increase in the third year, which is the year of tithing, then you shall give it to the Levite, to the foreigner, to the fatherless, and to the widow, that they may eat within your gates, and be filled.
verses.explainer: When you have finished setting aside a tenth of all your produce in the third year, the year of the tithe, you shall give it to the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, so that they may eat in your towns and be satisfied.

verses.text: You shall say before Yahweh your God, “I have put away the holy things out of my house, and also have given them to the Levite, to the foreigner, to the fatherless, and to the widow, according to all your commandment which you have commanded me. I have not transgressed any of your commandments, neither have I forgotten them.
verses.explainer: Then say to the Lord your God: 'I have removed from my house the sacred portion and have given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the fatherless and the widow, according to all you commanded. I have not turned aside from your commands nor have I forgotten any of them.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: This is a 'negative confession' -- declaring what you have not done wrong. Similar declarations appear in Egyptian funerary texts (the 'Weighing of the Heart'). Here it is not a judgment of the dead but a living person's accountability before God.

verses.text: I have not eaten of it in my mourning, neither have I removed any of it while I was unclean, nor given of it for the dead. I have listened to Yahweh my God’s voice. I have done according to all that you have commanded me.
verses.explainer: I have not eaten any of the sacred portion while I was in mourning, nor have I removed any of it while I was unclean, nor have I offered any of it to the dead. I have obeyed the Lord my God; I have done everything you commanded me.

verses.text: Look down from your holy habitation, from heaven, and bless your people Israel, and the ground which you have given us, as you swore to our fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey.”
verses.explainer: Look down from heaven, your holy dwelling place, and bless your people Israel and the land you have given us as you promised on oath to our ancestors, a land flowing with milk and honey.

verses.text: Today Yahweh your God commands you to do these statutes and ordinances. You shall therefore keep and do them with all your heart and with all your soul.
verses.explainer: The Lord your God commands you this day to follow these decrees and laws; carefully observe them with all your heart and with all your soul.

verses.text: You have declared today that Yahweh is your God, and that you would walk in his ways, and keep his statutes, and his commandments, and his ordinances, and listen to his voice.
verses.explainer: You have declared this day that the Lord is your God and that you will walk in obedience to him, that you will keep his decrees, commands, and laws -- that you will listen to him.

verses.text: Yahweh has declared today that you are a people for his own possession, as he has promised you, and that you should keep all his commandments.
verses.explainer: And the Lord has declared this day that you are his people, his treasured possession as he promised, and that you are to keep all his commands.

verses.text: He will make you high above all nations that he has made, in praise, in name, and in honor; and that you may be a holy people to Yahweh your God, as he has spoken.
verses.explainer: He has declared that he will set you in praise, fame, and honor high above all the nations he has made and that you will be a people holy to the Lord your God, as he promised.

explainer.summary: Moses commands that when Israel crosses the Jordan, they must set up large stones coated with plaster and write the law on them on Mount Ebal. An altar of uncut stones is to be built there for burnt offerings. The Levites then pronounce twelve curses -- covering idolatry, dishonoring parents, moving boundary stones, leading the blind astray, denying justice, sexual sins, secret murder, and failure to uphold the law. The people respond 'Amen' to each curse.
themes: The law inscribed on stones for public display
An altar of uncut stones
Twelve curses covering hidden sins
The people's corporate 'Amen'
Covenant ceremony at Gerizim and Ebal
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 27 begins the covenant renewal section (chapters 27-30). The curses address sins that are typically committed in secret -- precisely the sins that no human court can detect. They put the offender under divine judgment.

wordStudies.significance: Each curse begins with 'arur' (cursed). The word places the offender under divine judgment. The twelve curses parallel the twelve tribes, suggesting that the entire nation is covered by this comprehensive catalog of forbidden behavior.

verses.text: Moses and the elders of Israel commanded the people, saying, “Keep all the commandment which I command you today.
verses.explainer: Moses and the elders of Israel command the people: keep all these commands that I give you today.

verses.text: It shall be on the day when you shall pass over the Jordan to the land which Yahweh your God gives you, that you shall set yourself up great stones, and coat them with plaster.
verses.explainer: When you have crossed the Jordan into the land the Lord is giving you, set up some large stones and coat them with plaster.

verses.text: You shall write on them all the words of this law, when you have passed over; that you may go in to the land which Yahweh your God gives you, a land flowing with milk and honey, as Yahweh, the God of your fathers, has promised you.
verses.explainer: Write on them all the words of this law when you have crossed over to enter the land the Lord is giving you, a land flowing with milk and honey, just as the Lord, the God of your ancestors, promised you.

verses.text: It shall be, when you have crossed over the Jordan, that you shall set up these stones, which I command you today, on Mount Ebal, and you shall coat them with plaster.
verses.explainer: And when you have crossed the Jordan, set up these stones on Mount Ebal, as I command you today, and coat them with plaster.

verses.text: There you shall build an altar to Yahweh your God, an altar of stones. You shall not use any iron tool on them.
verses.explainer: Build there an altar to the Lord your God, an altar of stones. Do not use any iron tool on them.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 20:25 -- The prohibition against using iron tools on altar stones goes back to Sinai. Cut stone was considered 'profaned' by human tools.
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 20:25 -- The prohibition against using iron tools on altar stones goes back to Sinai. Cut stone was considered 'profaned' by human tools.

verses.text: You shall build Yahweh your God’s altar of uncut stones. You shall offer burnt offerings on it to Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Build the altar of the Lord your God with fieldstones and offer burnt offerings on it to the Lord your God.

verses.text: You shall sacrifice peace offerings, and shall eat there. You shall rejoice before Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Sacrifice fellowship offerings there, eating them and rejoicing in the presence of the Lord your God.

verses.text: You shall write on the stones all the words of this law very plainly.”
verses.explainer: And you shall write very clearly all the words of this law on these stones you have set up.

verses.text: Moses and the priests the Levites spoke to all Israel, saying, “Be silent, and listen, Israel! Today you have become the people of Yahweh your God.
verses.explainer: Then Moses and the Levitical priests say to all Israel: 'Be silent, Israel, and listen! You have now become the people of the Lord your God.'

verses.text: You shall therefore obey Yahweh your God’s voice, and do his commandments and his statutes, which I command you today.”
verses.explainer: Obey the Lord your God and follow his commands and decrees that I give you today.

verses.text: Moses commanded the people the same day, saying,
verses.explainer: On the same day Moses commands the people: when you have crossed the Jordan, these tribes shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The six tribes for blessing are the sons of Leah and Rachel -- the primary wives. The six tribes for cursing are the sons of the secondary wives plus Reuben and Zebulun. The arrangement reflects the tribal hierarchy.

verses.text: “These shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people, when you have crossed over the Jordan: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.
verses.explainer: These tribes shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people: Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.

verses.text: These shall stand on Mount Ebal for the curse: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.
verses.explainer: And these tribes shall stand on Mount Ebal for the curse: Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.

verses.text: With a loud voice, the Levites shall say to all the men of Israel,
verses.explainer: The Levites shall recite to all the people of Israel in a loud voice:

verses.text:
‘Cursed is the man who makes an engraved or molten image, an abomination to Yahweh, the work of the hands of the craftsman, and sets it up in secret.’
All the people shall answer and say, ‘Amen.’
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is anyone who carves an image or casts an idol -- a thing detestable to the Lord, the work of skilled hands -- and sets it up in secret.' Then all the people shall say, 'Amen!'

thenVsNow.then: The first curse addresses secret idolatry -- setting up an image 'in secret' where no court can detect it. The curse places such offenders under divine judgment since human justice cannot reach them.
thenVsNow.now: The emphasis on secret sins throughout the twelve curses addresses the gap between public compliance and private behavior. God sees what human courts cannot.

verses.text:
‘Cursed is he who dishonors his father or his mother.’
All the people shall say, ‘Amen.’
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is anyone who dishonors their father or their mother.' Then all the people shall say, 'Amen!'

verses.text:
‘Cursed is he who removes his neighbor’s landmark.’
All the people shall say, ‘Amen.’
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is anyone who moves their neighbor's boundary stone.' Then all the people shall say, 'Amen!'

verses.text:
‘Cursed is he who leads the blind astray on the road.’
All the people shall say, ‘Amen.’
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is anyone who leads the blind astray on the road.' Then all the people shall say, 'Amen!'

verses.text:
‘Cursed is he who withholds justice from the foreigner, fatherless, and widow.’
All the people shall say, ‘Amen.’
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is anyone who withholds justice from the foreigner, the fatherless or the widow.' Then all the people shall say, 'Amen!'

verses.text:
‘Cursed is he who lies with his father’s wife, because he dishonors his father’s bed.’
All the people shall say, ‘Amen.’
